exercises.name: Here Comes Leslie
exercises.description:
Stand in a circle and build a cumulative story about Leslie's increasingly silly actions.

  • First player says "Here comes Leslie walkin down the street" while miming the action
  • All players repeat the line and mime together
  • First player asks the next player "what's Leslie doin?" and that player adds a new action with mime
  • All players repeat the full sequence so far, adding each new mime action in order
  • Continue around the circle, with each player adding one new action to remember and mime
exercises.description_raw:
Here Comes Leslie

By learnimprov | April 16, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

What’s Johnny Doing?

Introduction

Please form a circle for a sing songy story thing.

Description

This is a very structured story that is more like spoken word than it is sung. The warm up challenges the players listening and remembering skills. It can also be used to encourage a bit of mime. Since there is no way to convey rhythm or meter easily we will just show the structure. Each action should be accompanied with a mime action as well.

  • First player “Here comes Leslie walkin down the street.” while miming walking on the spot.
  • All players “Here comes Leslie walkin down the street.” All mimicking the first player’s walk.
  • First player, questioning the next player, “what’s Leslie doin?”
  • Second player responds, “Leslie’s skipping to a beat” while miming the skipping.
  • All players “Here comes Leslie walkin down the street. Skippin to a beat.” Carrying out the associated mime actions.
  • Second player questioning next player, “what’s Leslie doin?”
  • Third player responds, “Leslie’s jugglin a frog” with the appropriate mime action.
  • All players “Here comes Leslie walkin down the street. Skippin to a beat. Jugglin a frog.” Carrying out the associated mime actions for each action.

This continues until all the players in the circle have contributed. Yes Leslie’ actions become harder and harder to remember. The players can use the mime actions to help cue the next activity for Leslie. Mime objects and mime actions do not make things harder. Mime actions and objects are great memory tools.

The leader can complicate the warm up by speeding things up, requiring each activity to rhyme, insist on zany unrelated mime actions. The highest level of complication is having the players use cross circle instead of just going around the circle

Gimmicks

  • Zany activities and/or zany mime.

Variations

  • None

Credits

  • Information appreciated.
Category: Mime Warm-Up Tags: circle, mime, narrative
exercises.summary: Each player adds one silly action to Leslie's walk while the group repeats and mimes the growing sequence together.

exercises.name: Try That on For Size
exercises.description:
Organize into a circle. The leader gives the first player a simple mime action like brushing teeth. That player mimes the action, then turns to the next player and makes a bold statement redefining what they're doing while continuing the same mime, like "I am putting on lipstick, try that on for size." Each player passes the action around the circle with a new justification until the mime action becomes impossible to justify.

  • Start with a simple mime action and pass it clockwise around the circle
  • Each player continues the exact same physical action but gives it a completely different purpose
  • Keep going until someone breaks or the justifications run out
  • Embrace the failure—the goal is to laugh and fail with grace
exercises.description_raw:
Try That on For Size

By learnimprov | April 15, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

None.

Introduction

Please organize into a circle.  

Description

This is a warm up that tests the players ability to come up with justifications for a mime action. The leader will endow a player with a simple mime action like “brushing teeth.” This warm up is like high jumping it will always end in failure. As with most warm ups the goal is to fail with grace and laughter.

The first player will mime brushing their teeth. They then turn to the next player in the circle and make a bold statement saying that they are doing something other than brushing their teeth while continuing the same mime action. For example, “I am putting on lipstick, try that on for size.” The next player continues the same mime action turns to the next player in the circle and says “I am polishing my braces.” This continues until the mime action is exhausted.

Gimmicks

  • Start players with wild and large action or small unrecognizable action.

Variations

  • Can be done in lines, pairs etc.
  • Altering the mime object each time so that players can explore new mime actions.

Credits

  • Found on youtube
Category: Listening Mime Warm-Up Tags: circle, mime object
exercises.summary: One mime action travels the circle as each player redefines its purpose with bold new justifications until logic breaks down.

exercises.name: Convergence
exercises.description:
Players form a circle and take turns in pairs stepping forward to simultaneously say a word they're thinking of, aiming to match.

  1. First player steps into circle and says "I have a word." Second player immediately steps in and says "I have a word."
  2. Both players face each other, count down together "Three, two, one," then simultaneously speak their chosen word aloud.
  3. After speaking, players reveal what word they actually said, even if it sounded garbled or didn't match.
  4. Two new players step forward and repeat the process. Success happens when both players say the same word simultaneously.
  5. Continue with fresh pairs cycling through until convergence occurs or the group decides to stop.
exercises.description_raw:
Convergence

By learnimprov | April 15, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

None.

Introduction

Please form a circle.

Description

Convergence is a warm up that focuses on listening, support, taking risks and getting a group in synch. The goal is to get two players to simultaneously say the same word without preparation.  When it happens it is magical, however it can take a long time to happen.

The warm up starts by having one player step into the circle and proclaim, “I have a word.” A second player will immediately step into the circle and proclaim, “I have a word.” The two players face each other, count to three and simultaneously say the word that they are thinking of. The players need to speak simultaneously and most often no one will understand the word they said, unless they both say the same word. It is good for the players to recap their words after they have spoken simultaneously. It could sound like this.

  • Player One “I have a word.”
  • Player Two “I have a word.”
  • Both players count down “Three, two one”
  • Both players at same time “walzuruchini!” (may sound like this)
  • Player One “I said walrus.”
  • Player Two “I said zucchini.”

The process continues with another two players stepping into the circle, proclaiming “I have a word” and repeating the process. The goal is to have the next players simultaneously say the same word. This would be considered a success. The players have been primed to listen to the words and to try and find a single word the converges the previous concepts. For example, if players were to call out words like limousine, space ship or laptop one would wonder how this would lead to a convergence of words. The warm up is a good opportunity to point of the benefits of not trying to be funny, and letting the comedy happen by accident. While naughty words can be funny, they do not necessarily contribute to the goal of the exercise. Convergence also helps players work with expansion versus explosion. Small expansions of the words given is more likely to be successful and is good practise for making small expansions on ideas in scenes. So the second wave could sound something like this

  • Player Three “I have a word.”
  • Player Four “I have a word.”
  • Both players count down “Three, two one”
  • Both players at same time “sesalidion!” (may sound like this)
  • Player Three “I said sea lion.”
  • Player Four “I said salad.”

There is a lot of failure in convergence. New players must step forward and commit to a word knowing that they are most likely not going to hit the goal. It can take a while for the group to converge. As the process goes on there is much opportunity to fail with grace and laughter. Encouraging the group to move faster with their guesses helps with commitment to their ideas and accepting that while there is a framework, there is no script.

  • Player Five “I have a word.”
  • Player Six “I have a word.”
  • Both players count down “Three, two one”
  • Both players at same time “sea cucumber!”
  • There is much rejoicing.

Sometimes this convergence feels like it is never going to happen. The players will cycle through rotation after rotation. The leader can use this opportunity to point out how close the ideas are but they are never exactly in synchronization. This is often the best that a connection can be during scene. Yet the players will soldier on remaining positive and working together. Improv comedy is most often the process of looking for success.

Some groups will automatically converge every round. They are clearly mutants with telepathy, and they should be reported to the appropriate local authorities.

Gimmicks

  • None

Variations

  • Two Hander – Pairs of players instead of a circle
  • Elimination – For a large circle players that don’ match sit down or step out of the circle

Credits

I knew you were going to say that.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: circle, listening, pairs, synchronization
exercises.summary: Two players simultaneously speak a word they're thinking of, trying to match while the circle watches and celebrates any convergence.

exercises.name: Fish Story
exercises.description:
Players stand in a circle passing imaginary fish of different sizes, alternating between big and small while miming accurate hand spacing.

  • First player says "big fish" with hands far apart, next player accepts and says "small fish" with hands close together
  • Continue alternating around the circle, maintaining consistent sizing for each fish type
  • If a player says one size but mimes another, or repeats the same size twice, circle calls "Fish Story" and that player casts their line to restart with a different player
  • Leader can add complications: "really big fish," "really small fish," reversed sizing, or sea creatures like squid or whale to increase difficulty
exercises.description_raw:
Fish Story

By learnimprov | March 21, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Big Fish Small Fish.

Introduction

Please form a circle and prepare to go fish.

Description

In this warm up the players will make a comment on a fish size and hold their hands out in front of them like they are holding a fish. The fist player will turn to the next player in the circle and say “big fish” holding their hands apart like they are demonstrating the size of a big catch. That player accepts the fish by miming their hands the same distance apart. That player turns and says “small fish” to the next player holding theirs a distance apart to indicate a small fish. That player accepts the small fish, turns to the next player and says “big fish.

If a player were to accept a big fish and turn to give a big fish to next player this is a Fish Story (aka a fib). All the players in the circle call out “fish story.” That player must look to the center and cast their fishing line out to another player and that player starts the circle again. If a player were to call out “big fish” and mime a small fish that would warrant the circle to call out “fish story.” If the player makes a hand spacing that is grossly different from the first big fish or fist small fish dimensions, then the circle should call out “fish story.”

Initially this exercise will sound like “big fish, small fish, big fish, small fish…” The leader can add complications to get the circle to call out Fish Story more often. The leader can add in “really big fish” with wider hand spacing. The leader can add in “really small fish” with even closer hand spacing. The leader can Where ever the leader adds in the additional fish sizes creates the cycle. It could sound like this “big fish, really big fish, small fish, big fish, really big fish, small fish…” If a player makes an error the circle calls out “fish story,” and that player casts across the circle.

Complications can continue. The leader can call ask the players to reverse the size of the fish. So “big fish” becomes a small fish sizing, and “little fish” becomes big fish sizing. If a player messes this up then “Fish Story!”

If they are getting bored other sea life can be added in. “Squid” is accompanied by wiggly hands trying to hold a struggling squid. “Whale” requires all the players to run into the circle to help hold up a whale. Keep the same sequence, increase the complications until they are failing with grace and laughter. f

Gimmicks

  • Insert a crazy sea creature that the players must create hand signals for.

Variations

  • Competitive – Players that fib about their fish size are out of the circle.

Credits

None.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: fishing, lying
exercises.summary: Players pass alternating sized imaginary fish around a circle, calling out sizes while miming accurate hand spacing to avoid contradictions.

exercises.name: Categories-Warm Up
exercises.description:
Players stand in a circle and rapidly call out items from a chosen category, then build a pointing sequence that becomes increasingly complex.

  • Team leader selects a broad category like food, cars, or toys
  • Players call out offers around the circle while one player points to the next speaker, creating a sequence
  • Once memorized, repeat the sequence without pointing, using only eye contact and listening
  • Add additional categories with new sequences, then layer them together
  • Progress with complications like eyes closed or distractions to deepen listening and commitment
exercises.description_raw:
Categories-Warm Up

By learnimprov | March 12, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

None.

Introduction

Please form a circle.

Description

Categories is both an exercise, handle and warm up. This is the warm up version of the structure.

The team leader will guide the players in choosing a broad category. For example, cars, toys, cutlery, food, etc. The players will be tasked with coming up with offers from the category. While the exercise has many ways to administer Categories the warm up has but one.

The players call out offers from the category around the circle. If the category was food it could sound like “dinner”, “turkey”, “ice cream”, etc. As time is spent on the category it become more challenging to come up with new ideas. The players should be encouraged to not repeat ideas.

In Categories, the warm up, once a player has called out their offer she will point, and keep pointing, to another player in the circle. That player then calls out her offer and points to another player. Players cannot point at a player who is already pointing. Eventually every player in the circle will be pointing at another player.

This process is repeated using the same offers and the same order of pointing. Once the players feel that they have the sequence memorized they are ready for complications.

The first complication is to repeat the sequence without pointing. The sequence will sound exactly the same but the cues are given by making eye contact and listening for the offer that preceded yours. It is a bit harder to do.

The next complication is add another category into the mix. This new category will have a new set of offers and a different order of pointing. Once this new sequence is memorized they leader will combine the two categories such that a category sequence of food could be happening at the same time as a category sequence of cars.

The leader can keep adding complications like more categories, eyes closed, or other distractions. Like any good warm up this one fails with grace and laughter.

This warm up focuses on two main elements of LACE (Listen. Accept. Commit. Expand.) The player must be listening to both words and phsyical cues. The player must also commit to their offer and get used to making mistakes.

Gimmicks

  • None.

Variations

  • None.

Credits

None.

Category: commitment failure Listening Teamwork Warm-Up Tags: categories, circle, prop, puns
exercises.summary: One player points around a circle calling out category items, then layers memorized sequences together while removing visual cues.

exercises.name: Big Blob
exercises.description:
Players start clumped on the right side of the stage. One at a time, walk across to the other side while navigating an imaginary blob of goo blocking your path. The leader describes the blob's density, viscosity, and visibility to shape how you move through it.

  • First player enters the blob and changes their gait to match the material—lean, push with arms, move in slow motion, or struggle through
  • Each following player crosses through the same blob, matching the established movement quality
  • For a challenge, send two players simultaneously; they must coordinate their movements to show they're in the identical blob
  • Advanced: endow the blob with emotions, taste, or smell to deepen the physical exploration
exercises.description_raw:
Big Blob

By learnimprov | March 2, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Honey Walk.

Introduction

Please clump together on the right side of the stage.

Description

Big Blob is both an exercise and a warm up. This is the warm up version of this structure. The leader asks each player to walk across the stage to the other side. However, in their path is a giant blob of goo. The leader should describe a big blob of goo blocking the path of the player’s walk across the stage. The blob will have density, viscosity, visibility endowments that will impact how the players move through the blob.

The first player will enter the blob and change their gait to reflect the nature of the materiel. The player could lean into the goo, they may move in slow motion, they may need to push through with their arms. The next player moves across the stage through the same blob. The following players will endeavour to make it appear that they are moving through the exam same blob. The leader should encourage the players to make it appear that they are pushing their way through the same blob.

A complication for this warm up is to have more than one player walk toward the blob at the same time. They start with a normal gait and the two players must work together to create the same blob. If one player is moving slowly the other player must as well. If one player has the blob pushing them down the other player will follow because they are both in the same blob.

A complication for more experienced players is to endow the blob with emotions or more complex elements like taste and smell.

Gimmicks

  • None

Variations

  • Honey Walk – very focused version of big blob using oxygen masks and honey.

Credits

None.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: character, environments, walking
exercises.summary: One player at a time crosses stage through an imaginary blob whose physical properties are described to shape each person's distinct movement quality.

exercises.name: Circle of Knots
exercises.description:
Players stand in a circle holding hands while one designated player weaves between them, creating an increasingly tangled knot without anyone releasing their grip.

  1. Form a circle and hold hands with neighbors
  2. One player gently moves under, over, and between other players' arms
  3. Remaining players shift hand positions but never let go
  4. Continue until the group forms a complex human knot
  5. Optional: work together to untangle without releasing hands
exercises.description_raw:
Circle of Knots

By learnimprov | February 16, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

None.

Introduction

Please join in a circle holding hands.

Description

Once in a circle a player is designated to gently move between players in the circle creating a Circle of Knots. The players do not let go of each other, but they may shift hand positions. The result is a complicated mess of players.

Gimmicks

  • None.

Variations

  • Chain Reaction.

Credits

Picked this up from Improv Encyclopedia.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: circle
exercises.summary: One player weaves through a hand-holding circle to create a human knot that everyone must untangle without breaking grip.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:
Introduction

Please join in a circle holding hands.

Description

Once in a circle a player is designated to gently move between players in the circle creating a Circle of Knots. The players do not let go of each other, but they may shift hand positions. The result is a complicated mess of players.

Variations

  • Chain Reaction.

exercises.name: Chain Reaction
exercises.description:
Players mill around the room in Brownian motion, then connect by placing their left hand on another player's left shoulder. Hands and shoulders bond permanently, forming molecular chains and circles that continue moving together.

  • Start milling freely around the room with both arms at sides
  • Raise your left hand when signaled and continue milling
  • Find a left shoulder to rest your left hand on—each shoulder holds only one hand, and hands cannot break once connected
  • Move as bonded molecules and circles around the room; unconnected players (free radicals) can join by finding available shoulders
  • Repeat with right hand on right shoulder, or advance to both hands on two different players' shoulders
exercises.description_raw:
Chain Reaction

By learnimprov | February 16, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

None.

Introduction

Please start milling about the room simulating Brownian Motion.

Description

The leader should alert the players that this exercise will involve shoulder touching and likely close contact with other players. Players should feel safe and supported if they choose not to participate.

Once the players are milling about the room the leader asks them to raise their left hand and mill for a bit longer. When the chain reaction is started each player must find a left shoulder to rest their left hand on. A shoulder can only support one hand at a time. A left hand cannot rest on a right shoulder. The hand shoulder bond should not break after being connected. When working this will form collections of players that have formed into molecules. The molecules keep milling about the room.

The chain reaction is a bit chaotic, but it is not competitive, so there is no need for players to run looking for a shoulder to connect with.

The warm up looks slightly frenetic while the hand shoulder bonds are being made. After a few moments it settles into a random collection of circles and pairs of players bonded together. Occasionally there will be a free radical (unconnected player). The larger molecules should avoid the urge to break bonds and absorb them into the circle. Once stabilized the players return to milling and switch to a right hand right shoulder configuration.

The leader can complicate this warm up by having players raise both hands. When the chain reaction starts the players must find a shoulder for their left and right hands, but they cannot rest on the same player. So the player’s left hand must be on one player and her right hand on another player. Complicated molecules are formed.

The Chain Reaction can take place with more energy (faster) or less energy (slow motion). The nature of the reactions can be endowed with emotions. The leader can also encourage the players to have a sound effect that they make while milling. Once the molecule is formed encourage the group of players to find a new shared noise to make.

This is a warm up that allows for several teaching opportunities. The leader can encourage the players to have fun and not be competitive. It is good to accept that player may become a free radical. The group can also demonstrate supportive behaviour by moving their free shoulders towards unconnected hands.

Gimmicks

  • Switch from left hand left shoulder to left hand left ankle.

Variations

  • Circle of Knots

Credits

Your reactions are helpful.

Category: support Teamwork Warm-Up Tags: chemistry, milling
exercises.summary: Connected players form permanent molecular chains by linking left hands to shoulders while milling, creating growing structures that move together.

exercises.name: Ball Pass
exercises.description:
Form a circle facing inward. One player receives an imaginary ball with specific attributes like size, weight, and form, then passes it left around the circle.

  • Each player receiving the ball must match its established properties—shape, weight, texture, and any other qualities
  • Work together to make the mime object feel genuinely real through committed physicality
  • Leader can transform the ball as it circulates—changing weight, size, smell, or emotional qualities
  • Optional: introduce a second ball moving right, or add new balls mid-exercise
exercises.description_raw:
Ball Pass

By learnimprov | February 16, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

None.

Introduction

Please form a circle facing inwards.

Description

A player is given a ball by the leader. The type of ball is determined by the player. They may go with a standard sports ball or create some other ball shaped object. The ball should have the following attributes: size, weight, and form. The players do their best accepting the ball being passed to the left. The players must work together to make the mime object ball seem real. When receiving the ball, they must match it’s shape, it’s weight, it’s size and any other attributes endowed to the mime object. Time is taken and attention to detail is paid. The leader may need to encourage players to make the ball as real as possible. The leader can start to transform the ball as it moves around the circle. Making it heavier, smaller, smellier, or endow it with sadness or joy.

It is fair game to add an additional ball in to circle.

Gimmicks

  • Add another ball going in the opposite direction.

Variations

  • Clay – mouldable blob passed from player to player

Credits

If you are on the ball please pass it along.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: ball, circle, mime object
exercises.summary: An imaginary ball circulates around a circle while players commit to its established physical properties and transformations.

exercises.name: Clay
exercises.description:
Stand in a circle and silently mold invisible clay into objects for others to identify and transform.

  1. First player shapes clay with their hands, adding emotion or smell but no explanation
  2. Pass the mime object left; receiver acknowledges its weight and shape before using it
  3. Receiver mashes it back into a ball and creates a new object
  4. Continue around the circle until everyone has created an object
  5. Players may naturally begin transforming each other's objects further
exercises.description_raw:
Clay

By learnimprov | February 16, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Plasticine. 3D Printer.

Introduction

Please form into a circle. You may wish to put on some mime gloves.

Description

One player is selected to form a small mime object from their ball of clay. This should appear like the player is molding modeler’s clay but there is no clay to be seen. The player does not speak about the clay. The player may inform it with a smell or an emotion but does not reveal what it is. Once the clay has been formed the player passes it to their left. The player receiving the mime object acknowledges it by respecting it’s weight, shape and use. They then proceed to mash it back into a ball. They create a wholly new object and pass it to their left. This continues until every player has created a mime object.

This warm up can be used to focus the players on what a mime object can represent in a scene. A mime object can have narrative purpose beyond it’s material form. Mime objects can be bestowed with emotions like love, resentment, or fear. Most groups will naturally start to play with each other’s objects. If this does not happen organically it is good teaching opportunity.

Gimmicks

  • None.

Variations

  • Ball Pass – ball of differing characteristics is passed around the circle.
  • Object Circle – mime objects are not mutable and pass around the circle.

Credits

Kitchener Waterloo Clay and Glass Museum.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: circle, clay, mime object
exercises.summary: Silently shape invisible clay into objects around a circle, passing each creation left for others to transform and reshape.

  • Object Circle – mime objects are not mutable and pass around the circle.

exercises.name: Object Circle
exercises.description:
Stand in a circle facing inward and silently pass mime objects around.

  • Each player creates a mime object using only their hands, showing how it's used through detailed physical actions
  • Make eye contact and silently pass your object to the player on your left
  • The receiving player observes carefully, then either imitates or adds to what they received before passing it on
  • When the object returns to its creator, discuss how it changed as it traveled around the circle
exercises.description_raw:
Object Circle

By learnimprov | February 16, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Mime Object. Clay. Ball Pass.

Introduction

Please form a circle facing inwards and mime an object with your hands.

Description

The leader encourages each player to create a mime object in her hands. The object is created by thinking of a mundane object and using it in with their hands. For example, a coffee cup could be mimed with one hand holding a handle and the other hand being warmed against the cup. The player could also sip from the cup. The sipping could give clues as to whether the fluid in the cup is warm, too hot, spicy, sweet or soothing. Basically if the player is  miming a cup of coffee anyone looking at the player would want a cup of coffee. They should be able to imagine the physical space occupied by the cup. This is one of an infinite number of examples. However, each player need not mime a different object. Don’t be concerned if multiple cups, notepads and cell phones are in the circle.

Once each player has created their mime object, they will silently pass it to the player on their left. The players must make eye contact and spend a bit of time observing the mime object. For example, the player passing the mime object to the left may mime sipping from the cup before passing it along. No words are spoken. The player on the left will acknowledge the received mime object by either imitating what the player mimed or by adding to it. For example, the player receiving the cup of coffee would mime the shape of the cup and acknowledge that it is hot as well. Remind the players that this exercise is stronger when the mime objects are passed silently around the circle.

Eventually the mime object will make its way back to the player and then the group has a fun debrief. The leader can ask the players what they received back. Was it the same cup? Did it have the same weight or colour or flavour? It is also fun to go around the circle following a mime object and asking players what they thought they had been given.

Teaching opportunities about here. This warm up is not just about creating mime objects it is about listening, accepting, and commitment as well. Players will frequently have no idea what the mime object is that they are receiving. They must practise committing to the unknown. The player must take this unknown object and pass it to the player to their left. This can be done my mimicry or exasperation, but they must commit to their mime. They need not worry about failure. Sometimes a mime object will not be understood by three players in a row and then the fourth player gets it. Players must also accept that their precious mime object could turn from a cup into a salamander. One accepts this wonderful new offer with grace and laughter. Firstly and lastly the player must listen with their eyes and mind. No mime object will survive if it is not listened to.

Gimmicks

  • Have a confidant in the circle intentionally pass the same mime object to their left.

Variations

  • Clay – each player creates something from a ball of clay passes it left and that player creates something new.
  • Ball Pass – all players are passing a ball that is endowed with new attributes as it moves around the circle.

Credits

Peruvian Ball Pass was set to paper by Augusto Boal.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: circle, mime object
exercises.summary: Silent mime objects travel around a circle, transforming through each player's interpretation before returning to their creator.

exercises.name: Pirates
exercises.description:
Players form a scraggly line facing different directions. A leader calls out pirate commands, and players perform the corresponding actions together, then return to the line to wait for the next call.

  • AHOY! – stand on one leg and salute, respond "aye aye"
  • HIT THE DECK! – drop to ground and scrub, make scrubbing sounds
  • OCTOPUS! – two players wiggle together as eight-limbed octopus, say "blub blub blub"
  • RAISE THE SAIL! – three players pull rigging together, chant "heave ho heave ho"
  • LAND AHOY! – four players mime rowing a boat to shore, call out "thar be gold"
  • WALK THE PLANK! – all hop with hands and ankles bound
  • BURY THE TREASURE! – all dig together
  • HOIST THAT CHEST! – four or more lift chest together, grunt "urgh and ugh"
exercises.description_raw:
Pirates

By learnimprov | February 16, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Overboard. Silly Pirates.

Introduction

Please create a scraggly line facing in different directions.

Description

This warm up is physical. Make sure that players feel safe and comfortable participating within their ability. Encourage players to use chairs, or other players as necessary.

The leader will explain the following commands and actions to the players. Once all the actions have been explained the players may call them out in the language of the pirate. The players fulfill the command and return to the only to wait for another.

  1. AHOY! – stand on one leg and salute. Players call out “aye aye.”
  2. HIT THE DECK! – get to the ground and scrub the deck. Players make scrubbing sounds
  3. OCTOPUS! – two players combine to create a wiggly 8 limbed octopus. The octopus can go “blub blub blub.”
  4. RAISE THE SAIL! – three players combine to pull on sail rigging. The team calls out “heave ho heave ho.”
  5. LAND AHOY! – four players get together and mime a boat rowing to shore. While rowing together they call out “thar be gold”

This warm up is aimed at getting players to share a setting through by combining mime objects, actions and chants. Teaching points here are reminding players to fail with grace and laughter.

There is no limit to the number of commands that can be thrown at the pirates. It is also fun to make one up once in a while to see how the group compensates. It is great opportunity for showing support, listening and honing ensemble skills.

  • WALK THE PLANK! – all the players hop around as if their hands and ankles are bound.
  • BURY THE TREASURE! – all the players start to dig.
  • HOIST THAT CHEST! – 4 or more players lift a chest extolled “urgh and ugh.”

Gimmicks

  • None.

Variations

  • Bunny Bunny warm ups are similar

Credits

Any help would be gold matey.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: arrgh, commands, line, pirates
exercises.summary: A leader calls pirate commands that send groups scrambling to perform synchronized physical actions before returning to the line.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:

Please create a scraggly line facing in different directions.

Description

  • Bunny Bunny warm ups are similar

exercises.name: Yes
exercises.description:
Players stand in a circle facing inward. One player makes eye contact with another across the circle and points. When acknowledged with "yes," the pointer crosses toward them while the acknowledger finds a new player. Continue so one person is always crossing the circle.

  • Form a circle facing inward
  • Player points and makes eye contact; other player says "yes"
  • Pointer moves across circle while acknowledger finds next player
  • Progress by removing pointing, then "yes," then nodding—eventually silent eye contact only
  • Embrace mistakes as learning moments for graceful failure
exercises.description_raw:
Yes

By learnimprov | February 16, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Cross Circle. You.

Introduction

Please form a circle facing inwards.

Description

Yes is a variation of cross circle that does not involve name learning. A player starts by looking across the circle and making eye contact with another player and pointing at them. When the player notices (hopefully right away) they will say “yes.” When the player pointing is acknowledged by another player she may start to move across the circle towards that player. The player that just said “yes” must now find another player in the circle by making eye contact and pointing. It is important for the leader to encourage the players NOT to move until they have been given a “yes.” When working well this warm up has one person continuously crossing the circle and sounds like yes-yes-yes.

The next complication is to remove the pointing. Players only make the connection by eye contact. Once eye contact is made the player will say “yes” and only then can the player start moving across the circle. This is quite a bit more challenging than pointing to get someone’s attention. When working well this warm up has NO pointing, and there is one person continuously crossing the circle. The warm up sounds like yes-yes-yes.

The next complication is removing the verbal acknowledgement of making the connection. The player acknowledges by silently nodding instead of saying yes. Now we have a warm up where players are nodding and silently crossing the circle.

The next complication is to remove the head nodding and players are silently moving across the circle acknowledge each other with what ever method they choose.

By this point there will be mistakes and mess ups happening. Players will be occasionally pointing or saying yes. This is completely fine. This is the point of this warm up. It is complicated so that players learn to fail with grace and laughter. All failures will be supported by the group as well. In improv there are no rules or errors. It is up to the group to recover by being supportive and carrying on.

If the circle is still rocking this warm up more complications can be added. For example, overlay the warm up Digits, or Word At A Time to mess things up.

Gimmicks

  • Start two or more players moving at once.

Variations

  • Cross Circle
  • Start with names and pointing

Credits

You know any?

Category: Listening Warm-Up Tags: circle
exercises.summary: One player crosses the circle while another finds the next target, creating continuous flowing movement through eye contact and verbal acknowledgment.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:

Description

exercises.name: Open Your Hands
exercises.description:
Players mill around the room and catch imaginary objects falling from the sky, then name what they caught before continuing to move.

  1. Everyone mills about freely in the room
  2. Leader calls out and all players mime catching something in their open hands
  3. Each player announces what fell into their hands, then discards it and resumes milling
  4. Leader can add qualities (heavy, sticky, cuddly) or require two players to catch and agree on one object through mime only
  5. Repeat the catch-and-name cycle
exercises.description_raw:
Open Your Hands

By learnimprov | February 15, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Pennies From Heaven.

Introduction

Start milling about the room and get ready to catch things falling from the sky.

Description

When the leader calls for it ever player in the room mimes catching something into their open hands. They then sound off what fell from the sky into their hands. Once a player has described what fell into their hands, they discard the mime object and continue to mill about.  This cycle of mill, catch, explain continues. The leader may start to endow the objects that fall from the sky. They can be heavy, smelly, sticky, cuddly, of soft.  The leader can add the complication that the object falling this time requires two players to catch it. The player must then agree on what the object is. They communicate by mime and try to avoid discussing what the mime object is.

This is not just a mime object warm up. There are lots of opportunities to discuss how a mime object affects the player, how it can be given weight or even emotion. This is also commitment warm up. The players are called on to literally create something from nothing. It is a teaching moment where players can be reassured that their choices are not wrong, and that they will be supported in them.

Gimmicks

  • Have them catch esoteric objects like democracy or an abyss..

Variations

  • None.

Credits

Created by Keith Johnstone. 

Category: Warm-Up Tags: catching, milling, mime object
exercises.summary: Milling players catch imaginary falling objects, name them aloud, then discard and continue moving through the space.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:
Introduction

Description

The player must then agree on what the object is. They communicate by mime and try to avoid discussing what the mime object is.

exercises.name: Making Faces
exercises.description:
Three players stand in a line facing the group, with a titled leader in the middle and servants on either side.

  • The middle player (queen, king, CEO) gives the servants tasks while they obey respectfully
  • When the leader looks left, the right servant mocks them with exaggerated faces and gestures
  • When the leader looks right, the left servant mocks them
  • Servants escalate their mockery until caught, then get replaced by a new player from the group
  • Continue until everyone has had a turn as leader
exercises.description_raw:
Making Faces

By learnimprov | February 15, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Saving Face.

Introduction

Three players lined up facing the rest of the players.

Description

The player in the middle is given some important title, like king or CEO. The players on either side are doing the middle players bidding. For purposes of this explanation we will have a Queen in the middle with two servants on her left and right. The queen will engage these servants with various servant like tasks. However, there are some respect issues in this monarchy. When the queen is looking left the player on the right is mocking her. When the queen is looking right the player on her left is mocking her. The goal for the servants is to make greater and greater irreverent gestures and faces until they get caught. When the queen catches a player mocking her the player is fired and replaced. This continues until the queen is dead. Long live the queen.

The goal of Making Faces is to have fun. It is also about commitment to the faces that the players make. They should not be competing, they should be failing and getting fired with grace and laughter.

Gimmicks

  • None.

Variations

  • Monarch.

Credits

Created by Keith Johnstone.  :

Category: Warm-Up Tags: line, silly, status
exercises.summary: A leader gives commands while hidden servants behind them escalate exaggerated mockery until caught and replaced.

exercises.name: Cat and Mouse
exercises.description:
Everyone spreads out in pairs throughout the room. The leader selects one pair to split into a cat and a mouse. The mouse escapes by joining any other milling pair, linking arms with one player. When the mouse links in, the person on the other side of that pair becomes the new cat, and the old cat becomes the new mouse. The chase continues with roles constantly shifting.

  • Start in pairs spread across the room
  • Cat chases mouse around the milling pairs
  • Mouse links arms with any pair to escape
  • The third person in that pair becomes the new cat
  • Chase restarts with new roles
exercises.description_raw:
Cat and Mouse

By learnimprov | February 15, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

None.

Introduction

Can everyone get into pairs and spread out about the room.

Description

The leader selects on pair to be split into a cat and a mouse. The cat wants to be with the mouse, but the mouse does not want to be with the cat. This will lead to a room full of slowly moving pairs and a cat chasing a mouse around the room. Eventually the mouse will be caught and the cat and mouse join back in as a pair.  The mouse still does not want to be with the cat. The mouse may reach out and join any pair that is milling around the circle.

When the mouse hooks arms with another player the mouse is free from the clutches of the cat. The cat is now alone and becomes a mouse. The third person, i.e., the extra person in the mouse pair bond becomes the cat. The new cat now starts to chase the new mouse (formerly a cat). This leads to a continuous cat and mouse chase.

The warm up is about having fun, getting moving, following instruction and offering support.

Gimmicks

  • None

Variations

  • Any prey and predator.

Credits

Could you chase something down please?

Category: Warm-Up Tags: chase, milling, pairs
exercises.summary: One player chases another who escapes by joining a pair, displacing the opposite end person as the new pursuer.

exercises.name: Life Game
exercises.description:
Mill around the room and secretly choose your role: egg (normal pace) or sperm (brisk pace). Pick one other player as a barrier and one as your target gamete. Eggs try to keep the barrier between themselves and their chosen sperm; sperm move toward their chosen egg while avoiding the barrier. But roles can flip—eggs may chase sperm, sperm may flee eggs. The room becomes controlled chaos.

  • When a sperm catches an egg (or vice versa), they hug and join hands, floating together through the remaining action
  • Paired players stop chasing and avoiding; they're out of active play but stay in the space
  • Multiple players can cluster as others still perceive them as sperm or egg
  • Embrace the madness—no competition, just listening and silliness
exercises.description_raw:
Life Game

By learnimprov | February 15, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Body Guard.

Introduction

Start milling about the room for this complicated game of life.

Description

This warm up can involve physical contact and whimsical references to reproduction and contraception. If a player does not wish to participate, they must feel safe and supported to NOT participate. A player should never have to explain why they have chosen to not participate.

While the players are milling around the room the players will secretly decide if they are an egg or a sperm. Even though the players don’t advertise this it will become obvious as eggs will move at a normal walking pace and sperm can walk briskly. The player then must choose to one other player in the room to be a barrier. The player then must choose another player to be a gamete other than their own.  An egg would choose a barrier and a sperm. A sperm would choose a barrier and an egg. In the initial version of this game the egg will move to keep the barrier between themselves and the sperm. The sperm would move towards the egg and avoid the barrier. However, life is more complicated than this.

The egg may choose to avoid the barrier and move towards the sperm. The sperm may choose to avoid the egg and try to keep the barrier between itself and the egg. Since no one will have any idea what anyone else is thinking the room should be full of a chaos of movement.

If a sperm catches up with an egg, they will give their perceived egg a hug and both the players are out of the warm up. This works the same if an egg catches a sperm. The resulting sperm players will join hands and just float joyfully amongst the chaos. It does not matter if two sperms join up or two eggs join up they will cease to avoid or chase. Yes players can clump together in groups as there are still other players who perceive them as either a sperm or an egg.

This warm up is a mad house. The goals for this warm up include the usual ice breaker qualities as well as listening to the room, acceptance by not making it a competition, and just being silly.

Gimmicks

  • None.

Variations

  • Body Guard.

Credits

Please implant your knowledge upon us.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: chase, egg, fertility, milling, sperm
exercises.summary: Milling players secretly become eggs or sperm, using barriers and targets to chase or evade until pairs hug and float together through the chaos.

exercises.name: Body Guard
exercises.description:
All players mill freely around the room. Each player secretly picks one other player as their bully and a different player as their bodyguard.

  • Keep moving and milling without running
  • Position yourself so your bodyguard is always between you and your bully
  • Try not to reveal who your bully or bodyguard is through your movements
  • Continue until patterns become obvious and roles are clear
exercises.description_raw:
Body Guard

By learnimprov | February 15, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Assassin.

Introduction

Please start milling about the room.

Description

Each player secretly picks a bully and a bodyguard from amongst the other players. The bully and the bodyguard must be two different players.  The leader checks to make sure that every player has selected a bully and a bodyguard. The players will try to avoid the bully by moving so that the bodyguard is between the bully and themselves. Do not run. Encourage the players to continue milling and try not to let on who is their bully or their bodyguard. This warm up can continue until it is clear who are the bullies and the bodyguards.

This warm up is the standard version of a huge subset of triangulation structures. Most are covered under Variations below. The goal of these warm ups is to get players moving about the room. The simple complication of trying to position oneself in relation to two other players is unique. This is a good warm up for listening to the room. Body Guard widens the gaze of the player to include the whole room and not just the face to face listening. It is also a fun ice breaker.

Gimmicks

  • Bully, bodyguard and police. The player wants to stand beside the police and have the bodyguard between themselves and the bully.  
  • Slow motion or sped up.

Variations

  • Valentine’s Game – Players choose one player that is a crush, and one that is an ex. Moving towards the crush but away from the ex.
  • Stinky – Players choose one player that is smelly, and one that is a deodorant. Keeping the deodorant between themselves and smelly.
  • Life Game – Players can be an egg (slow moving) or a sperm (fast moving). The player can choose to use the barrier as something to avoid or use as a block.

Credits

Please share this guarded information.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: chase, milling
exercises.summary: Players mill around while secretly positioning themselves so their chosen bodyguard stays between them and their bully.

exercises.name: Name Volley
exercises.description:
Two groups face each other across an imaginary volleyball net. Players mime volleys while calling out names to direct the ball.

  • Server calls a name on the opposite side and mimes serving to that player
  • Receiver can bump to a teammate by saying their name, or return over the net by naming an opponent
  • Make eye contact before calling names and mime the ball direction clearly
  • If stuck, call "help" and a teammate supplies the name you then call out
  • Once names are learned, add complications: eye contact only, change ball qualities (heavy, light, hot), or alter the environment (beach, snow, pool)
exercises.description_raw:
Name Volley

By learnimprov | February 14, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

None.

Introduction

Set up into two groups on either side of a volley ball net.

Description

The players are going to play a non-competitive round of volley ball where they call out names from the players on the other side of the net. So when a player goes to serve the ball she must call the name of a player on the other side. That player will receive the ball. She can bump it to a player on her team by saying that name or return it over the net by saying the name of a player on the other team. All of this is mimed with lovely volley ball actions. If a player cannot think of a name, they call help and another player will call out their name. The player that needed help calls that name and directs the ball to them.

The leader should encourage the players to make eye contact before they call the players name. Also, the mime should reflect the direction that the ball will go. This will keep the group from randomly calling names and failing to associate them with a player. This is a name learning warm up after all.

The leader can add complications once the players have learned the names. The ball can be volleyed around by just eye contact. The ball can be assigned new qualities like heavy, light, hot, or pointy. The net can become higher, lower, opaque. The playing surface can be changed to a gym, a beach, deep snow or a pool.  These are helpful setting tools that explore both mime objects, and environment.

Gimmicks

  • None.

Variations

  • None

Credits

If you could serve us something we would bump it up here.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: names, setting, volleyball
exercises.summary: Two teams volley an imaginary ball across a net by calling out each other's names to direct play.

exercises.name: One To Ten
exercises.description:
Mill around the room in a neutral state while the leader calls out emotions and intensity levels from one to ten.

  1. Start milling naturally as the leader assigns an emotion and a number (happy at 3, angry at 7, etc.)
  2. Embody that emotion at the specified intensity through your movement, facial expression, and energy
  3. When the leader calls a new emotion or level, shift your physicality accordingly
  4. Explore how the same emotion changes dramatically at different intensities (sad at 2 versus sad at 9)
  5. After several rounds, discuss what physical and vocal elements shifted at each level
exercises.description_raw:
One To Ten

By learnimprov | February 14, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Energy 1 to 10. Emotional Scales

Introduction

Please start milling about the room in a neutral state.

Description

The leader calls out some endowment for the entire group. It could be an occupation, an animal type, but most commonly it is an emotion. The players embrace this endowment changing how they mill around the room. The leader should choose general emotions like angry, or sad.

A scale is assigned to the emotional endowment. So happy 1 would be a bit happy. No longer neutral. A low key happy that may show slightly on the face, or in the gait. Happy 5 would be obviously happy, a bit louder, a much more buoyant gait. Happy 10 would be over the top loud raucous joy. The leader will call out different emotions and different levels. What is a plumber at level 10? Each group will explore that differently.

The leader should take the opportunity to discuss what elements players felt were present at different emotional levels.

Gimmicks

  • Leader assigns crazy levels like 42 or -5.
  • Leader chooses esoteric complex emotions like: lust, apathy, or confusion.

Variations

  • None.

Credits

Any info 1 to info 10 would be appreciated.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: emotions, milling
exercises.summary: Milling actors embody emotions at varying intensity levels called by a leader, exploring how physicality transforms across the emotional spectrum.

exercises.name: One Duck
exercises.description:
Stand in a circle and build a rhythmic counting game that gets progressively harder until the math breaks down.

  1. Player one calls "one duck," player two calls "two legs," entire circle quacks twice.
  2. Player three calls "two ducks," player four calls "four legs," entire circle quacks four times.
  3. Continue around the circle, doubling each time: next player says the number of ducks, next player multiplies by two for legs, whole circle quacks that many times.
  4. Keep going until someone miscounts or the quacking becomes impossible to track—embrace the failure together.
  5. Optional: try variations like "one centipede, 100 legs" or "one spider, eight legs" with different sounds.
exercises.description_raw:
One Duck

By learnimprov | February 14, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

None.

Introduction

Please form a circle for a rhythm and team work warm up.

Description

This is a rhythm exercise with changing parameters. Failure is imminent. One player calls out one duck. The next player calls out two legs. All players go “quack quack.” The next player calls out two ducks. The next player calls out four legs. The entire circle goes quack quack quack quack. The quacks for the entire circle equal the total number of legs. Got it? This continues until the math gets too hard. Initially it is recommended that the players work in sequence so that eventually it sounds like “8 ducks”, “16 legs”, followed by 16 quacks.

The group usually fails when there are too many quacks to count on 10 digits. It is also possible for a player to faulter when multiplying by two. The leader will remind the players that failing with grace and laughter is one of the essential parts of improv. The reality is that most failures will be group failures.

Gimmicks

  • One centipede, 100 legs, scurry scurry scurry…
  • One snake, no legs, hisss.
  • speed up the rhythm

Variations

  • One pirate, one leg, “arrrgh”
  • One beetle, six legs, “scuttle scuttle scuttle scuttle scuttle scuttle”
  • One spider, eight legs, “scitter scitter scitter scitter scitter scitter scitter scitter”

Credits

One datum of info, one thank you, thanks!

Category: Warm-Up Tags: circle, ducks, failure
exercises.summary: Build escalating rhythmic patterns by doubling ducks and quacks until the math collapses into joyful chaos.

exercises.name: Head and Shoulders
exercises.description:
Stand in a circle and establish a steady 1-2-3-4 rhythm together. Assign each number a movement: 1 is head touch, 2 is shoulder touch, 3 is hip slap, 4 is knee touch.

  1. First round: say all numbers aloud with no movements
  2. Replace 1 with head touch, say 2-3-4
  3. Replace 2 with shoulder touch, say 3-4
  4. Replace 3 with hip slap, say 4
  5. Perform all four movements silently with no speaking, then speed up until the group breaks down
exercises.description_raw:
Head and Shoulders

By learnimprov | February 14, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Head, Shoulder, Knee No Toes. 1-2-3-4.

Introduction

Please form a circle for a movement exercise designed for create failure.

Description

This is counting warm up where players replace numbers in the sequence with specific movements. Get the group finding a nice rhythm with everyone counting 1-2-3-4. Each number is associated with a specific movement.

  1. touch your head with both hands
  2. touch your shoulders with both hands
  3. slap your hips with both hands
  4. touch your knees with both hand

Once the group has associated the numbers with the movements the warm up can start.

  • First wave players say 1-2-3-4 with no moves. Repeat once
  • Second wave head touch replaces saying 1, and players say 2-3-4. Repeat once
  • Third wave head touch replaces 1, shoulder touch replaces 2, then players say 3-4. Repeat once
  • Fourth wave head touch for 1, shoulder touch for 2, hip slap replaces 3, then players say 4. Repeat once.
  • Fifth wave is all four movements and no speaking. Repeat once.

Players will usually get flummoxed by these senseless directions at some point. The leader can discuss that failing with grace and laughter is a key element of improvisation. It also allows the group to be supportive. With some groups this can be played as an elimination warm up. Speeding up the 1-2-3-4 rhythm each time a player is out of the circle.

Gimmicks

  • Go too fast
  • Add hard to do gestures, like slap left foot.
  • Add additional numbers and gestures.

Variations

  • None.

Credits

Heads up would be great.

Category: failure Listening Warm-Up Tags: circle, failure, rhythm
exercises.summary: A circle gradually replaces spoken numbers with synchronized movements, accelerating until the group's rhythm collapses.

exercises.name: Satellite TV
exercises.description:
Partners establish a rhythm saying "earth-orbit-earth-orbit" back and forth. Player orbit then replaces "orbit" with a new slow movement each beat while player earth mirrors that movement with a one-beat delay, creating a delayed visual echo effect.

  • Stand in pairs facing each other
  • Establish rhythm: say "earth-orbit-earth-orbit" together
  • Player orbit adds one new movement per beat instead of saying "orbit"
  • Player earth watches and repeats each movement one beat behind
  • Swap roles when the sequence becomes too chaotic
exercises.description_raw:
Satellite TV

By learnimprov | February 14, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

None.

Introduction

Please get into pairs for a listening your eyes warm up.

Description

The players work together form a rhythm repeating earth-orbit-earth-orbit back and forth. Once the rhythm is nicely established player orbit starts to move. They will move slowly adding a new movement each beat, instead of saying orbit. Player earth will then repeat the movement that player had just made. The theory is that someone watching will see two simultaneous image streams that are the same but slightly delayed. Visual data delay is a phenomena of satellite communications.

The warm up is difficult for both players. Player orbit must not be distracted by player earth’s movements may struggle to keep a sensical series of actions. Player earth must watch with her eyes while moving at the same time. Once they decay into a jumbled mess of gesticulations (usually due to a solar flare) the players swap roles.

Gimmicks

  • Player orbit moves fast.
  • Combine with Satellite Radio.

Variations

  • Satellite TV – the same warm up except player orbit is offering a series of movements that are copied with appropriate delay.

Credits

Any help would be out of this world.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: tv
exercises.summary: One partner initiates slow movements while the other mirrors them one beat behind, creating a cascading echo effect.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:

exercises.name: Satellite Radio
exercises.description:
Partners establish a steady rhythm saying "earth-orbit-earth-orbit" together, then one player begins adding new words on each beat while the other echoes with a one-beat delay, creating two simultaneous stories.

  • Stand in pairs facing each other
  • Build rhythm by alternating "earth" and "orbit" until steady
  • Player orbit stops saying "orbit" and instead adds one new word per beat to form a slow story
  • Player earth repeats each word orbit just said, staying one beat behind
  • Swap roles when the words become too jumbled to continue
exercises.description_raw:
Satellite Radio

By learnimprov | February 14, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

None.

Introduction

Please get into pairs for a listening warm up.

Description

The players work together form a rhythm saying earth-orbit-earth-orbit. Once the rhythm is nicely established player orbit starts to talk. They will speak slowly adding a new word each beat, instead of saying orbit. Player earth will then repeat the word that player had just said. The theory is that a listener will hear two simultaneous stories that are the same but slightly delayed. Voice delay is a phenomena of satellite communications.

The warm up is difficult for both players. Player orbit must not be distracted by player earth’s words and will struggle to keep a sensical story. Player earth must listen while speaking. Once they decay into a jumbled mess of words (usually due to a solar flare) the players swap roles.

Gimmicks

  • Player orbit talks fast
  • Combine with satellite tv.

Variations

  • Satellite TV – the same warm up except player orbit is offering a series of movements that are copied with appropriate delay.

Credits

Any help would be out of this world.

Category: Focus Listening Warm-Up Tags: radio
exercises.summary: One player builds a story word-by-word while their partner echoes each word one beat behind, creating overlapping narratives from a shared rhythm.

exercises.name: Body Hide
exercises.description:
Two players stand far apart with the remaining players positioned between them as a living screen to hide one player from the other's view.

  • Assign one player as the hider and one as the spotter, standing at a distance from each other
  • All other players are bushes who stay near the hider and move to block the spotter's line of sight
  • The hider and spotter cannot move closer or further apart, only contort their bodies
  • If the spotter sees the hider, a bush becomes the new hider and swaps positions
  • If the spotter cannot see anything, remove one bush who joins the spotter or moves behind the hider
exercises.description_raw:
Body Hide

By learnimprov | February 14, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Camouflage. Eye Spy.

Introduction

We need two players standing far apart and all remaining players between them.

Description

One player is assigned the role of hiding.  The other player is assigned the role of spotter. The rest of the players are the bushes. The bushes must stay near the hider and position themselves in such a way to block the spotter’s view. The spotter and the hider cannot move in relation to each other. They can contort themselves but cannot get closer or further apart. Only the bushes can move.

It is not fair play to have the bushes run up and cover the spotter’s eyes. The bushes must remain near the hider.

If the spotter should be able to see the hider through the bushes that hider is caught, a player from the bush becomes the hider and the hider joins the spotter. If the spotter declares that she cannot see anything one of the bushes is removed. The bush may join the spotter or become background bushes behind the hider. This continues until the group runs out of bushes. If one the player that has joined the spotter can see the hider, they can usurp the spotter.

This warm up works best when it is not a competition. The goal is having the bushes work together to create a visual barrier. The warm up should naturally be fluid, with different spotters and hiders. The leader can shake things up if necessary.

Gimmicks

  • None.

Variations

  • None.

Credits

If you spot something let us know.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: bushes, hiding
exercises.summary: One player hides behind moving "bushes" while another tries to spot them without changing distance.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:
Introduction

exercises.name: Eights
exercises.description:
Find your own space in the room where you won't contact others. Progress through rotating body parts in figure-eight motions, starting small and building to larger movements.

  1. Stand on your left foot and rotate your right big toe in a figure eight, then your whole right foot
  2. Continue with your right leg, then hips, shoulders, head, arms, elbows, and thumbs
  3. Use chairs, walls, or lean on others as needed—there's no correct way to move
  4. The leader can call out body parts or invite players to choose what rotates next
  5. Keep going until you feel limber and comfortable moving in unusual ways
exercises.description_raw:
Eights

By learnimprov | February 14, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Circle of Eights.

Introduction

This is a physical warm up. Please find a spot in the room where you won’t contact another player.

Description

This is simple warm up that each player can try within their physical ability. If you prefer to use a chair or lay on the floor that is fine.

Players start out by simply rotating their right big toe in a figure of eight motion. Next the players start to move their entire right foot in a figure of eight motion. The room should have players standing on their left foot and rotation their right foot in gentle figure eights. Then the players rotate their entire right leg in a figure of eight. Move through the rest of the body: hips, head shoulders, thumbs, arms elbows. The leader can call on players to choose body parts to rotate. The warm up continues until the players feel limber.

There is no correct way to rotate one’s body part. Players will be laying down, holding walls, chairs, or leaning on each other. The goal of this warm up is to get one in sync with their body and familiarize the players with moving in unnatural ways in a public space. The leader could introduce discussion topics while figure of eighting: favourite improv goals, character ideas, good things that happened, etc.

Gimmicks

  • Rotating internal organs.

Variations

  • Pieces of Eight – when standing on one leg the players make pirate “arrrgh” noises.

Credits

Information appreciated.

Category: Warm-Up
exercises.summary: Rotate individual body parts in figure-eight patterns, progressing from toes to head to build flexibility and movement awareness.

exercises.name: Tug of War
exercises.description:
Two lines face each other holding an invisible rope, working together to make it look real through synchronized effort and responsive mime.

  1. Form two equal lines facing off with space between them
  2. Both groups mime gripping and pulling the rope, responding to each other's effort
  3. Leader watches for realistic physics—if one side pulls harder, the other responds with strain
  4. Leader can endow groups as winning, add fatigue to players, or make the rope break to shift formation
exercises.description_raw:
Tug of War

By learnimprov | February 14, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

None.

Introduction

Create two lines facing off for a tug of war.

Description

Both groups mime the rope between them as one group. There is no competition between the groups. The goal is to make the rope look as real as possible When one group mimes extra effort the opposing group mimes a response to that effort. The leader must pay attention to whether the rope is stretching too much, or defying material physics. The leader can endow one group to be winning or endow a certain players to become sleepy.

This is both a physical warm up and way to show the benefits of ensemble work. The warm up works best when every player is miming the best rope they can.

Gimmicks

  • The Rope Breaks
  • Move players to make it lopsided

Variations

  • None

Credits

Contributions appreciated.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: lines
exercises.summary: Two lines mime pulling an invisible rope, synchronizing effort and responding to each other's physical strain.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:
Introduction

Description

exercises.name: Alliteration Occupation
exercises.description:
Players stand in a circle with one player in the center who points to circle players and assigns letters. Each pointed player must quickly create a sentence with a name, occupation, and location all starting with that letter.

  • Center player points to someone in the circle and calls out a letter
  • That player improvises a sentence with a name, occupation, and location using that letter (example: "Betty bakes bread in Boston")
  • Center player points to another circle player with a new letter and repeats
  • Rotate the center player every few rounds
  • Embrace mistakes and quick answers over perfect ones
exercises.description_raw:
Alliteration Occupation

By learnimprov | February 13, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Barney.

Introduction

Please form a circle with one player in the center. This is a spontaneity warm up about having fun with words.

Description

The player in the center will control the warm up by suddenly pointing to a player in the circle and assigning them a letter. That player must quickly put together a very specific sentence. The sentence must contain a name, an occupation, and a location. For example, if the player were assigned B their sentence could be Barney bakes bread in Baltimore. Once the player is done the center player calls on another player with another letter. Letters can be repeated. The player in the circle is periodically switched out by the leader every few letters.

This can be challenging for many players. Especially players that like to be funny or get things “right” The teaching opportunity here is the remind the players that improv comedy is about failing with grace and laughter. You will only be measured by your effort and commitment not your stand up funny skill. If a player is being overwhelmed by this warm up switching to gibberish can be helpful.

Gimmicks

  • Using letters like X, Z or Q.
  • Hit the same player repeatedly.
  • Repeatedly use the same letter over and over.

Variations

  • Some houses will replace location with a non-geographical location.
  • Play in pairs going back and forth with the alphabet.

Credits

Input welcome.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: circle
exercises.summary: Center player calls out letters while pointing to circle members who instantly create alliterative sentences with names, occupations, and locations.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:
Introduction

Description

exercises.name: Alliteration Prop
exercises.description:
Players stand in a circle and pass a prop while one player rapidly lists words starting with an assigned letter. When the prop returns, that player's turn ends and a new player gets a new letter.

  1. Form a circle and designate one player to start with the prop and a letter from the alphabet
  2. The player begins saying words that start with their letter while passing the prop left around the circle
  3. When the prop completes the circle and returns to the player, their turn ends
  4. Pass the prop to the next player, assign a new letter, and repeat
  5. Track word counts if desired, but emphasize support through passing speed and graceful failures
exercises.description_raw:
Alliteration Prop

By learnimprov | February 13, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Categories.

Introduction

Let’s form a circle for a word game warm up.

Description

The leader introduces a prop into the circle. It is recommended that the prop be both safe to toss and indestructible. Most commonly used is a tennis ball. The player holding the prop is assigned a letter from the alphabet. The player passes the prop to her left and immediately starts to say as many words that start with the assigned letter. For example if the player was assigned the letter P she it would sound something like: pebble, poppy, pulse, pregnant, purple. While she is listing off these words the prop is being passed around the circle. When the prop reaches her she is done. The prop is handed to another player who is assigned a new letter and the process starts over.

Learn Improv is not big on competitive warm ups, however this is one that usually becomes competitive. Players may pass the prop  around the circle really fast and keep track of how many words the player comes up with. The leader can use this as a teaching opportunity for discussing the goal of support. Passing the prop slowly is supportive. Passing the prop quickly can also be supportive if the player is completely stuck. Players will often be self critical during this warm up. The leader can remind everyone that musch of improv is about failing with grace and laughter.

Reconsider this warm up if there are players with linguistic challenges in the group.

Gimmicks

  • Assign brutal letters like X and Z

Variations

  • Categories can be done the same way.

Credits

Please help.

Category: Characters Problem Warm-Up Tags: circle, words
exercises.summary: One player rapidly names words starting with an assigned letter while passing a prop around the circle until it returns.

exercises.name: Alliteration Introduction
exercises.description:
Players stand in a circle and learn each other's names through progressive rounds of introduction, alliteration, and physical expression.

  1. Form a circle. Each player says their name once.
  2. Go around again. Each player adds a positive alliterative adjective to their name (like Brilliant Brian).
  3. Third round: players repeat their alliterative name and strike a pose that matches it.
  4. Finally, the whole circle repeats each player's name and mirrors their pose as that person performs it.
exercises.description_raw:
Alliteration Introduction

By learnimprov | February 13, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Super Me.

Introduction

This warm up will help us learn names. Please form a circle.

Description

Once the players have formed a circle each player contributes their name. After this first pass is done each player will repeat their name and add an alliteration that makes them sound fantastical. For example, Brilliant Brian, Excellent Eric, Super Susan. The leader should encourage the players to make positive alliterations about themselves. On the next pass the players repeat their super name and strike a pose that reflects the name. For example Super Susan may strike the classic flying super hero pose. Now everyone in the circle repeats everyone else’s magnified moniker and makes the appropriate maneuver.

Alliteration Introduction helps the players get a bit more comfortable with silly. This warm up also helps activate listening to words and actions simultaneously.

Gimmicks

  • None

Variations

  • The circle can repeat after the player each step lengthening the duration but improving retention.
  • Do everything at once alliteration and action.

Credits

Input appreciated.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: circle, ice breaker, intros, names
exercises.summary: Each person adds an alliterative adjective and pose to their name while the circle mirrors them back.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:
Introduction

This warm up will help us learn names. Please form a circle.

Description

For example, Brilliant Brian, Excellent Eric, Super Susan. The leader should encourage the players to make positive alliterations about themselves. On the next pass the players repeat their super name and strike a pose that reflects the name. For example Super Susan may strike the classic flying super hero pose. Now everyone in the circle repeats everyone else’s magnified moniker and makes the appropriate maneuver.

Alliteration Introduction helps the players get a bit more comfortable with silly. This warm up also helps activate listening to words and actions simultaneously.
