exercises.name: Walk By Numbers
exercises.description:
Players stand scattered throughout the room maintaining eye contact with one milling player who moves briskly around them. When the milling player stops, another seamlessly takes their place. Progress to two players moving in sync, stopping together, then two more starting simultaneously. The challenge is smooth transitions and graceful handling of timing mishaps.

  • One player mills around the room while others stand still and watch
  • When the milling player stops, another player immediately begins moving
  • Advance to two players moving together, stopping in unison
  • Two new players then start moving as the first pair stops
  • Embrace moments when multiple players start at once—find creative solutions together
exercises.description_raw:
Walk By Numbers

By learnimprov | February 13, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Group Still.

Introduction

This is listening warm-up. Everyone find a spot in the room.

Description

In some ways this warm up is the opposite of group stop.

The players stand like statues but keep an eye on each other. One players starts to mill around the room. Briskly moving in and out and around the players standing still. The player must try their best to maintain eye contact with the solitary milling player.

The milling player will suddenly stop. At that moment another player will start to move about the room in the same pattern. The goal is to seamlessly pass from one wandering player to another while those standing still keep watch.

The next complication is to assign two players to move about. They must stop in sync (like group stop) and two more players must seamlessly start to move about the room.

A common challenge is having multiple players start to move at the same time. This is a teaching opportunity to reinforce failing with grace and laughter. Let the players find their own way to accommodate too many players starting to move.

Walk By Numbers is a listening and commitment warm up. At first it is listening with the eyes, freezing when you see others freeze. At it’s highest level the ensemble will be listening with its heart and brain, knowing when to stop and start as one.

Gimmicks

  • Endow the players with strange gaits to impair listening
  • Endow the players with loud vocalizations that impair listening

Variations

  • None

Credits

None

Category: Less Listening Problem Teamwork Warm-Up Tags: ensemble, listening, standing
exercises.summary: One player mills while others watch, then seamlessly swap roles as the group coordinates smooth transitions between moving and still positions.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:
Introduction

Description

exercises.name: Big Brother
exercises.description:
Stand in a circle and assign one player Big Brother, then number everyone consecutively around the circle to their left.

  • Establish a four-beat rhythm saying "Big Brother Big Brother" followed by "Big Brother [number]"
  • The called number responds with "Big Brother [different number]" and play continues
  • When someone misses their number, everyone cheers "Oh Yeah!" and that player moves to Big Brother's right, becoming the highest number
  • Everyone shifts positions and renumbers accordingly, then restart the rhythm immediately
  • Big Brother can also call another Big Brother instead of a number; if Big Brother makes an error, they become the highest number and Number One becomes Big Brother
exercises.description_raw:
Big Brother

By learnimprov | February 11, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

The Prisoner. Famous deprecated name.

Introduction

Everyone get in a circle for a complicated competitive warm up.

Description

Big Brother is complicated. As in all competitive structures Big Brother is about failing with grace and laughter.

One player in the circle is assigned the title Big Brother. The player to her left is assigned the Number One, the player to her left is Number Two. This continues around the circle until every player has been assigned a number and one is assigned Big Brother. This is the easy part.

A rhythm is begun and it is worth practising a bit. The rhythm is a four beat to the tune of Big Brother Big Brother. Followed by Big Brother some number. They player with that number must follow with Big Brother another number. This continues until a player makes an error. Everyone calls out a supportive Oh Yeah, and that player heads to the right of Big Brother and assumes the largest number in the circle. This could mean that many of the players change their numbers. This makes the Big Brother Number something more difficult.

A painful example follows.

In a circle of 8 players there is Big Brother, to her left Number One, then Number Two all the way up to Number Seven to the immediate right of Big Brother.

Everyone starts the four beat with Big Brother Big Brother. Then Big Brother calls out “Big Brother number 4”, Number Four calls out “Big Brother number 3”, Number Three calls out “Big Brother number 6”, Big Brother Number Six calls out “Big Brother number 2”.

However Big Brother Number Two was thinking about Fairy Cakes and does not notice her number being called out. Everyone else in the circle notices and calls out Oh Yeah! Number Two is directed to the right of Big Brother and becomes Big Brother Number Seven. Big Brother Number Seven becomes Big Brother Number Six, Big Brother Number Six becomes Big Brother Number Five and so on. Once Big Brother and Big Brother Number One keep their titles.

Immediately after the Oh Yeah. Big Brother Big Brother starts again.

If you need to have a goal you can try and become Big Brother. Players can call out Big Brother Big Brother instead of Big Brother Number Four. If Big Brother slips up she becomes Big Brother Number Seven (in this example) and Big Brother Number One becomes Big Brother.

Phew.

The goal is to have fun. Warm up paying attention and learn to fail with grace and laughter while being supported by the other players.

Gimmicks

  • Please none.

Variations

  • Boom She Boom

Credits

Contributions appreciated.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: circle, competition
exercises.summary: One player calls numbers in rhythm while others respond and shift positions when someone misses their cue.

exercises.name: Sevens
exercises.description:
Stand in a circle and flap each limb while counting down from seven to one, building speed and energy with each round.

  1. Flap left arm into the circle while counting to 7, then right arm to 7
  2. Kick left leg into the circle 7 times, then right leg 7 times
  3. Repeat the full cycle counting down to 6, then 5, then 4, getting faster each time
  4. Continue through 3, 2, and finish with rapid 1-1-1-1 counts with maximum energy
  5. Optional: substitute other body parts like hips, head, or nose for variety
exercises.description_raw:
Sevens

By learnimprov | February 11, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Seven Up.

Introduction

Let’s get in a circle.

Description

All the players in the circle flap their left arms into the circle count with each flap and counting up to 7. Next they all flap their right arm into the circle times. Next their left leg kicks in 7 times and then their right leg 7 times.

The whole limb flapping cycle repeats with the group counting up to 6. Then 5. Then 4. The cycle gets faster and faster and finishing with a resounding 1-1-1-1. Lots of limb flailing fun.

Gimmicks

  • Throw in some different body parts: hips, head, nose.

Variations

  • None.

Credits

Contributions appreciated.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: circle, flailing
exercises.summary: Flap each limb in sequence while counting down from seven to one, accelerating speed and energy with every decreasing round.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:
Introduction

Let’s get in a circle.

Description

All the players in the circle flap their left arms into the circle count with each flap and counting up to 7. Next they all flap their right arm into the circle times. Next their left leg kicks in 7 times and then their right leg 7 times.

The whole limb flapping cycle repeats with the group counting up to 6. Then 5. Then 4. The cycle gets faster and faster and finishing with a resounding 1-1-1-1. Lots of limb flailing fun.

Gimmicks

  • Throw in some different body parts: hips, head, nose.

Variations

  • None.

exercises.name: Group Sort
exercises.description:
Players stand in a line facing the same direction and silently reorganize themselves by a characteristic called out by the leader.

  • Start in a single line all facing the same way
  • Leader calls out a characteristic like height, shoe size, or hair color
  • Players rearrange themselves silently and decisively without talking
  • Commit fully to your placement even if unsure—getting it wrong with confidence matters more than hesitation
  • For added challenge, try sorting with eyes closed using spotters for safety
exercises.description_raw:
Group Sort

By learnimprov | February 11, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Group Order. Line Up.

Introduction

Everyone please form a line all facing the same direction.

Description

Once everyone is lined up the leader will challenge the players to quickly and safely line up in an order based on a characteristic. The easiest one is to the group line up by height. This should be done silently and with commitment. It is better for the group to be incorrect and decisive.

The ordering can become more complicated: hair colour, shoe size, hat size or most frugal. If the leader knows the group well more personal characteristics may be challenged. Line up by order of most children, number of car accidents, bravery, or length of intestines. The warm up can be complicated further by cross indexing: number of X chromosomes and typing speed.

In the end it does not matter what the sort is. What matters is committing as a group and failing with grace and laughter.

Just don’t do weight. There are so many other characteristics to work with.

Gimmicks

  • None

Variations

  • Do the sort with eyes closed. Spotters recommended.

Credits

Picked this up from Improv Encyclopedia and sanitized.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: line, silly
exercises.summary: Performers silently rearrange themselves in line by called-out characteristics, committing fully to their placement without speaking.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:

exercises.name: Relaxations
exercises.description:
Stand in a circle in a comfortable spot. Choose from three relaxation techniques to calm and center the group before performing.

  • Four Square Breathing: Breathe in for 4 counts, hold for 4, exhale for 4, hold for 4. Repeat the cycle. Return to normal breathing if dizzy.
  • Contract and Relax: Tightly squeeze muscle groups (calves, buttocks, fists, shoulders, legs, face) for 4-5 seconds, then release. Notice how relaxation feels in each area.
  • Thoughtful Meditation: Imagine yourself in a peaceful place like a glen or beach. Explore it with your senses—notice light, wind, warmth on your skin.
exercises.description_raw:
Relaxations

By learnimprov | February 11, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Supernova.

Introduction

Find a comfortable spot and location in the room.

Description

Relaxation exercises are well shown to improve performance in all kinds of situations. This includes meditation, breathing, getting in the moment, muscular relaxation. Almost all warm ups get players laughing an energized. It is up to the leader to guess what kind of warm up is best.

There is no science here, but it seems that relaxation warm ups are better for shows, and energizing warm ups better for workshops. Your mileage may vary.

Four Square Breathing

This warm up is used to get the player into the moment and learn a tool to keep performance anxiety from winning out. Advise players that breathing is more important than the warm up. If they experience dizziness or any sense of distress simply return to normal breathing. When learning it is best to work as a group in a circle.

Everyone breathes in for a 4 count, holds their breath for a 4 count (full lungs), gently exhales for a 4 count, and then holds their breath for a 4 count (empty lungs). The cycle is continued.

Players can use Four Square Breathing anywhere and many players return to say they have used in their mundane lives.

Contract and Relax

This warm up can be done standing or lying down. Have the players contract and relax muscle groups focusing on how the muscles feel when relaxed. This gets players in touch with their muscle groups and gives a tool to combat muscular tension.

For example, tightly squeeze your calf muscles and hold that for 4 to 5 seconds. Then relax the muscle group. Pay attention to how a muscle group feels relaxed. Repeat with buttocks, fists, shoulders, both legs, face, etc.

Thoughtful Meditation

Have the players place themselves in a comfortable and quiet imaginary place, like a glen or a beach. Gently challenge them to inspect that space with their imagination. How does the light reflect? What does the wind feel like on the skin? The sun? Etc. This relaxation is the most common one to lose the players attention.

Gimmicks

  • Endless money making schemes.

Variations

  • Too many to imagine.

Credits

Power of Now is a practical guide to meditation for real life.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: circle, hippy dippy, milling
exercises.summary: Groups select from breathing, muscle tension, or guided imagery techniques to center themselves before performing.

exercises.name: What Has Changed?
exercises.description:
Partners work together to build observation and listening skills through simple physical changes.

  • Form pairs facing each other
  • One player closes their eyes while the other makes a change to their appearance or position
  • The observing player opens their eyes and identifies what changed
  • Switch roles and repeat
  • Beginners use obvious changes like removing shoes; advanced players make subtle shifts like moving a hand or adjusting posture
exercises.description_raw:
What Has Changed?

By learnimprov | February 11, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

None.

Introduction

Please get into pairs to warm up our listening skills.

Description

During this very simple listening exercise one of the players closes her eyes while the other player alters her appearance in some fashion. When ready the player opens her eyes and tries to spot the change.

This change can be massive and obvious for the purpose of braking the ice, building support or just having fun (removing a shoe, or jacket). More advanced players may make subtle changes that are harder to pick up (moving a hand or foot). For more advanced players this warm up can be used to accentuate characters and intent listening.

  • category setting
  • excerpt creation
  • tag additions
  • sharing restrictions

Gimmicks

  • Don’t change anything at all.

Variations

  • None.

Credits

Contributions appreciated.

Category: Listening support Warm-Up Tags: ice breaker, listening, pairs
exercises.summary: One player closes their eyes while their partner changes something physical, then they guess what shifted.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:

Variations

exercises.name: Fuzzy Bunny
exercises.description:
Stand in a circle and count upwards from 1, replacing multiples of 3 with "fuzzy" and multiples of 7 with "bunny." When both apply, say "fuzzy bunny."

  1. Start counting together: 1, 2, fuzzy, 4, 5, fuzzy, bunny, 8, fuzzy...
  2. When someone makes a mistake, the group laughs supportively and restarts at 1
  3. Once comfortable, add the rule: numbers containing both 3 and 7 (like 37) become "fuzzy bunny"
  4. Keep going to practice failing gracefully as a team
exercises.description_raw:
Fuzzy Bunny

By learnimprov | February 10, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Fuzzy Ducky.

Introduction

Let’s get in a circle for a warm up that focuses on failure of numeracy.

Description

Fuzzy Bunny starts out as a simple circle counting exercise. The players count upwards from 1. However this counting is complicated by the asking the players to replace any usage of the number 3 with the word fuzzy. This means the number 3, 6, 9, 12 and 13 would be replaced with the word fuzzy. The warm up would sound like this. 1-2-fuzzy-4-5-fuzzy-7 and so on. When, not if, an error is made the circle offers support, laughs and restarts at 1.

Once the players are developing a measure of success the next level of complication is thrown into the mix. Any usage of the number 7 is replaced with the word Bunny. This means that the numbers 7,14, 17, and 21 would be replaced by the word bunny. The warm up should now sound something like this: 1-2-fuzzy-4-5-fuzzy-bunny-8-fuzzy-10-11-fuzzy-fuzzy-bunny.

To ensure that this warm up practises failing with grace and laughter the next complication is added. Whenever a number is both fuzzy (has a 3 in it) AND bunny (has a 7 in it) the player must say fuzzy bunny all at once. So numbers like 21, 37 would be replaced with fuzzy bunny. No Learn Improv is not going to calculate any other fuzzy bunnies.

Learn to fail with grace and laughter. Support is exemplified from the group by picking right up at 1 with a smile.

Gimmicks

  • Fuzzy Ducky eventually results in a hilarious spoonerism

Variations

  • Use words other than Fuzzy Bunny to keep it fresh.
  • Use prime numbers other than 3 and 7.
  • Count down from 100 instead of up from 1.

Credits

Picked this up from Improv Encyclopedia and taught to love it by Scott.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: circle, failure, numeracy, support
exercises.summary: Count upward in a circle, replacing multiples of 3 with "fuzzy" and multiples of 7 with "bunny," restarting together when mistakes occur.

exercises.name: Tick Tock O’clock
exercises.description:
Players stand in a circle and pass "tick" and "tock" in alternating directions like clock hands, then introduce "o'clock" calls that redirect the flow and trigger "bong" counts.

  1. Form a circle. One player points left or right, says "tock." Next player points any direction, says "tick." Continue alternating sounds with changing directions.
  2. Any player can replace tick or tock by calling out a time (e.g., "4 o'clock"), pointing across the circle to a new player.
  3. That player responds with "bong" repeated for the hour number, then points to restart tick-tock flow in a new direction.
  4. Return to tick-tock rhythm until the next o'clock call interrupts.
exercises.description_raw:
Tick Tock O’clock

By learnimprov | February 10, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Clocks.

Introduction

This warm up works on listening. Please form a circle.

Description

Once the players are in a circle a brief explanation of analog clocks may be required.

As per all these circle exercises one player starts off by pointing to either their left or right and saying “tock.” The next player can point in either direction and say “tick.” The direction of the “hands” will be constantly changing, but the room will hear tick-tock-tick-tock.

Once the clock is tick tocking along it is time to introduce the next level. Any player can replace tick or tock with o’clock. The player yells out o’clock points across the circle and state some time o’clock: 4 o’clock, 8 o’clock, 11 o’clock. The player that was clocked then calls out “bong” like a clock ringing on the hour. That player chooses a direction by pointing. The the circle must then count off the correct number of bongs and then return to tick-tock-tick-tock.

When Tick Tock O’Clock is ticking along it will sound like this. Tick-tock-tick-tock-tick-tock-tick-tock-4 o’clock-bong-bong-bong-bong- tick-tock-tick-tock. Your milage may vary.

This warm up is an homage to the analog watch that warms up listening, attention, commitment and failure. Group failure is met with support and laughter.

Gimmicks

  • Military Clock – use 24 hour clock
  • Digital Clock – everyone is silent and then someone says something o’clock and everyone remains silent.

Variations

  • Time Dilation – speed up or slow down the clock.

Credits

Contributions appreciated.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: analog, circle, watch
exercises.summary: Alternating "tick" and "tock" passes around a circle get disrupted by "o'clock" calls that redirect flow and trigger rhythmic "bong" counts.

exercises.name: Let Me Help You!
exercises.description:
One player mimes a simple activity in the center of the circle while others watch. When ready, another player jumps in saying "Let me help you!" The miming player responds with "Thank you, I am ______ing" and names something completely different from what they were miming. The helper immediately starts miming this new activity. Continue until everyone has jumped in.

  • Form a circle with one player starting in the center
  • Center player mimes an obvious activity until it's clear
  • A circle player jumps in and offers help
  • Center player accepts and names a radically different activity
  • New player takes center and mimes the stated activity
exercises.description_raw:
Let Me Help You!

By learnimprov | February 10, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

What Are You Doing?

Introduction

This warm up helps with listening and getting in to the moment. Everyone into a circle.

Description

Let Me Help You! is an replacement variation of the deprecated warm up What Are You Doing?

Once the circle is formed one player goes into the circle and starts to mime a simple activity. Once the activity has been established one of the players from the circle jumps in and exclaims “Let me help you!” The player doing the mime responds with “Thank you, I am ______ing.” The goal for the player is to come up with a radically different task from what they were miming. For example, if the player appeared to be mowing a lawn they might say ‘filleting a soul.’ The player that joined in immediately starts miming the described activity (i.e., filleting a soul). This continues until all have tried the exercise.

The warm up here is mostly for the player that wants to help. They must quickly shed their preconceived idea of what the player was doing and then adapt to mime something completely different. This is a good way to warm up accepting on the fly.

Gimmicks

None

Variations

  • Copying – the player starts to mime along with the player. When they are endowed with the new mime activity they must transition gracefully to the new mime activity.

Credits

Canter for Reducing Exploratory Narrative Destruction

Category: Accepting Listening Problem Warm-Up Tags: circle, support
exercises.summary: One player mimes an activity while others jump in to help, but the mime names something completely different for them to do instead.

exercises.name: Clap Snap Stamp
exercises.description:
Get into pairs facing each other and establish a synchronized rhythm by alternating who says each number in a 1-2-3 count across the whole group.

  • Start with players calling out numbers in sequence: first player says "one," second says "two," first says "three," then repeat
  • Once the group is in sync, replace "one" with a clap
  • Replace "two" with a finger snap
  • Replace "three" with a foot stamp
  • Continue the clap-snap-stamp pattern, focusing on listening to each other and staying together
exercises.description_raw:
Clap Snap Stamp

By learnimprov | February 8, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

None.

Introduction

Get into pairs for a rhythm exercise.

Description

Players face each other and start to count to three one number at a time. Eventually these numbers will be switched to some repetitive actions.

Counting one number at a time means that the first player counts one, and the second player counts two and the first player they counts three. This means that each player will count off each number. Once the entire group is in sync (not just the pairs) with 1-2-3. Then the challenge increases.

Instead of counting out loud the number one will be replaced by a clap. The room will then sound like clap-2-3. One settled a finger snap replaces the number two. The room will settle start to sound like clap-snap-3. Eventually a foot stamp is added and there are not more numbers just clap-snap-stamp.

This warm up is about honing individual and group listening skills. Since the Clap Snap Stamp devolves into chaos often it is a good teaching opportunity to make players comfortable with failing gracefully.

Gimmicks

None.

Variations

  • Eyes closed.
  • Keep adding actions beyond 3. Clap, Snap, Stamp, Sniff etc.

Credits

Contributions appreciated.

Category: Warm-Up
exercises.summary: Pairs maintain a synchronized 1-2-3 rhythm by replacing numbers with clap, snap, and stamp sounds in sequence.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:
Introduction

Get into pairs for a rhythm exercise.

Description

Counting one number at a time means that the first player counts one, and the second player counts two and the first player they counts three. This means that each player will count off each number. Once the entire group is in sync (not just the pairs) with 1-2-3. Then the challenge increases.

exercises.name: Pass Yes
exercises.description:
Stand in a circle and pass synchronized "yes" statements around the ring in pairs.

  • One player faces a neighbor and you both say "yes" together while making eye contact and using exaggerated gestures to telegraph the timing.
  • The player who received the "yes" turns to face the next person in the circle and repeats with them.
  • Continue passing around the circle, building rhythm and synchronization through focus and telegraphing.
  • Once flowing smoothly, players can pass the "yes" back to the person they just received it from, reversing direction with good eye contact.
  • Add a second "yes" or other repetitive action once the circle maintains seamless rhythm and direction changes.
exercises.description_raw:
Pass Yes

By learnimprov | February 8, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

None.

Introduction

Get into a circle for a listening and focus warm-up.

Description

Pass Yes is a variation of the warm up Simulclap. Instead of the clapping the players simultaneously say “yes” together. To save a lot of cut and paste substitute “yes” with clap below.

One player starts off by clapping their hands (a singular clap), while facing another player. The player mimics the the starting player and the goal is to clap simultaneously. The player that received the applause then turns and claps simultaneously with the next player in the circle. This process is continued around the circle in a series of individual claps built by pairs of players.

This is not a competitive warm up. The players must work with each other by making eye contact with their clap partner and telegraphing an exaggerated clapping motion.

Once the simultaneous clapping is moving nicely around the circle the warm up is ready for the next level. A player may now opt to give the clap right back to the player they got it from. This is accomplished with good eye contact and telegraphing gestures. This will reverse the direction of the clapping.

When this is working well there is a clapping sound zipping around the circle occasionally changing direction.

Once the circle is passing the clap around and changing direction in a seamless manner the leader can add in a second clap, or some other kind of repetitive action listed under variations.

Simulclap is a good warm up for enhancing listening and taking focus. Simulclap also allows the group to work with failing in comfort and that there are no rules only goals. Group support can also be demonstrated by the group not losing their rhythm or pointing out missteps.

Gimmicks

Make all kinds of jokes about passing the clap, or getting the clap.

Variations

  • Clap Story – Instead of clapping the player contributes a word from a Word at a Time story. This is really difficult.
  • Lederhosen – Players simultaneously slap their ankles with their hands passing around this dance step.
  • Simulsnap – Snap fingers instead of clapping.
  • Silentclap – Make the gesture of a clap but keep the meat from connecting so no clap sound is created.

Credits

None

Category: Focus Listening Problem Warm-Up Tags: circle, rhythm, yes
exercises.summary: Synchronized pairs pass "yes" statements around a circle with exaggerated gestures and eye contact to build rhythm and timing.

exercises.name: Rhythm Circle
exercises.description:
Stand in a circle and establish a shared 8-syllable nonsense chant together, then layer in synchronized repetitive motions that travel around the group.

  1. Form a circle and agree on an 8-syllable chant like "All my ships are out at sea"
  2. Chant together until the rhythm is solid
  3. One player adds a repetitive motion (like tapping forehead) synced to the chant, then passes it right
  4. The source player adds two more distinct motions (like thigh slaps) that also travel right
  5. Keep the chant continuous while three separate motions circle around the group
exercises.description_raw:
Rhythm Circle

By learnimprov | February 8, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

None.

Introduction

This warm up is fun and will help us get silly and working together. Please form a circle.

Description

Rhythm Circle has two distinct parts. A chant and a series of repetitive motions.

The chant is a non-sense sentence totally about 8 syllables. Some examples are: All my ships are out at sea. Alley cat cat has a mouse with leaves. Once the group has gotten the rhythm of the chant a player will add a repetitive motion.

The repetitive motion may or may not be in sync with the chant. For example the player could tap their forehead 8 times in sync with the chant. This repetitive motion is then repeated by the player to their right. The source player then adds a brand new repetitive motion, slapping their thigh for instance. This action is passed to the right as well. Historically the source player will only contribute 3 repetitive actions.

At this point the circle should be repeating an uninterrupted non-sense chant, and three separate repetitive actions should be following each other around the circle. While it seems simple enough to listen intently to the player on one’s left rhythm circles often collapse into hilarity.

As with most warm ups Rhythm Circle is about getting a handle on comfortable failure, group synchronization and listening.

Gimmicks

Increase the speed.

Variations

  • All the bah bahs total 40 sheeps.
  • Usually a room will have it’s own favourite chant

Credits

Contributions appreciated.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: circle, rhythm
exercises.summary: Synchronized motions layer onto a shared 8-syllable chant and travel around the circle at different speeds.

exercises.name: Puking Dinosaur
exercises.description:
Players stand in a circle with space to move freely. One player starts with a subtle pose and quiet sound, then passes it to their right neighbor.

  • Each player accepts the pose and sound, then exaggerates both slightly bigger and louder before passing right
  • The gesture and noise grow increasingly wild as they travel around the circle
  • When exaggeration peaks and can't grow further, the leader calls stop and a new subtle pose begins
exercises.description_raw:
Puking Dinosaur

By learnimprov | February 8, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Pass the Peep. Exaggeration Circle.

Introduction

This warm up will help us get the body warmed up and get more comfortable about being silly. Please organize in to a circle.

Description

Ensure that the players have enough space to move their arms freely without clocking their neighbour in the head.

Puking Dinosaur is the same as an exaggeration circle except the players are crossing the circle with their unique gait and sound.

For an explanation of how to explain how to cross a circle check here.

A player will throw strike an subtle yet unconventional pose and create a corresponding sound. In Exaggeration Circle it is important that the initial pose be subtle and the noise quiet. The pose and noise are given as an offer to the player on her right. That player accepts the offer and immediately turns to her right recreating the pose and noise as best as possible. However the next player strikes a pose that slightly grander and bit louder than the one they were given.

This process should result in a noise and shape flying around the circle at that keeps getting louder and bigger. Eventually the gesture and noise will be altered so much that is cannot possibly be exaggerated. At the apex of exaggeration the leader calls stop and new subtle pose is struck.

Gimmicks

Start so huge that there is no where to go.

Variations

Exaggeration Circle – exaggerate around a circle.

Credits

None

Category: Characters Listening Warm-Up Tags: circle, dinosaurs, puke
exercises.summary: A gesture and sound escalate wildly around the circle until exaggeration peaks, then reset to subtle and begin again.

exercises.name: Exaggeration Circle
exercises.description:
Stand in a circle with enough space to move your arms freely. One player starts by striking a subtle, unconventional pose with a quiet corresponding sound, offering it to the player on their right.

  • The next player accepts and recreates the pose and sound as accurately as possible
  • That player then turns right and passes it on, but with a slightly grander pose and louder sound
  • Continue around the circle, each player amplifying the physicality and volume
  • When the gesture becomes too exaggerated to enlarge further, the leader calls stop and a new subtle pose begins

exercises.name: Pass the Peep
exercises.description:
Players stand in a circle with enough space to move arms freely. One player creates an unconventional pose and matching sound, then passes both to the player on their right.

  1. The receiving player accepts the offer and immediately recreates the pose and sound as accurately as possible.
  2. The gesture and noise travel continuously around the circle at increasing speed.
  3. Poses and sounds will naturally alter through repetition or intentional changes.
  4. Variation: occasionally throw the pose and sound across the circle instead of to the right.
exercises.description_raw:
Pass the Peep

By learnimprov | February 8, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Sound Circle. Pass Catch. Accepting Circle.

Introduction

This warm up will help us get the body warmed up and get more comfortable about being silly. Please organize in to a circle.

Description

Ensure that the players have enough space to move their arms freely without clocking their neighbour in the head.

A player will throw strike an unconventional pose and create a corresponding sound to go along with the pose. The pose and noise are given as an offer to the player on her right. That player accepts the offer and immediately turns to her right recreating the pose and noise as best as possible.

This process should result in a continuous noise and shape flying around the circle at high speed. Eventually the gesture and noise will be altered intentionally or by accident. Pass the Peep works best when the players try their best to copy the offer given to them. Radically changes make the spectacle less likely to impress.

Pass the Peep is a warm up in silliness and listening. The warm up should help the players accept being silly in front of others. Pass the Peep is like Pass Catch because it forces a player into a physical position and to make a noise they would never consider otherwise. This warm up can be used to help players create the move and sound parts of their characters.

Gimmicks

Physically impossible acts or noises.

Variations

Once in a while throw the pose and noise across the circle.

Credits

None

Category: Characters Listening Warm-Up Tags: circle
exercises.summary: Unconventional poses and sounds travel around a circle, mutating through repetition and intentional variation.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:
Introduction

This warm up will help us get the body warmed up and get more comfortable about being silly. Please organize in to a circle.

Description

Ensure that the players have enough space to move their arms freely without clocking their neighbour in the head.

exercises.name: Bing Bing Bong
exercises.description:
Stand in a circle holding hands with players on either side. Start by squeezing the hand of the player to your right, who squeezes their right hand to pass it on. When squeezing right, say "bing"; when squeezing left, say "bong." The leader can send multiple squeezes around the circle in different directions simultaneously.

  • Join hands in a circle
  • First player squeezes right hand and says "bing"
  • Each player passes the squeeze in the same direction by squeezing their right hand
  • Reverse direction by squeezing left hand and saying "bong"
  • Leader adds multiple squeezes going different directions until chaos builds
exercises.description_raw:
Bing Bing Bong

By learnimprov | February 6, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Squeezer. Hands. Hands Up

Introduction

Everyone into a circle and join hands.

Description

One player in the circle will start by squeezing the hand of the player on her right. That player will squeeze the hand of the player on her right. So if someone squeezes your left hand you squeeze your right hand. This will create a series of squeezes that hurtle around the circle. This is the simplest form of hands.

If a player has their right hand squeezed they will squeeze their left hand. This will cause the squeezes to hurtle in the opposite direction of the paragraph above. So we can have a series of squeezes moving to the right or a series of squeezes moving to the left.

When a player squeezes their right hand they say “bing”. When they squeeze their left hand they say “bong.” One can listen to the wave binging and bonging it’s way around the circle

The leader can keep adding squeezes into the circle. There could be a squeeze heading to the right, with another squeeze following behind. There could be a squeeze going to the right and left at the same time. Usually the circle will keep binging and bonging until chaos reigns.

This is an ice breaker warm up that focuses on listening with out words and concentration. Since it usually ends in a chaotic squeezing of sweaty palms Hands also reminds players that failure is okay, and group failure is even more okay.

None.

Variations

  • Gate Controlled – the player cannot start their squeeze until the hand squeezing theirs has completed. This can change the rate of the squeeze moving around the circle.
  • Hands Up – Instead of holding hands the players put their palms up over their heads and push on their neighbour’s hand instead of squeezing. One can watch this push wave travel around the circle.

Credits

Learn Improv made this one up in the 1990s.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: bing, bong, circle
exercises.summary: Hand squeezes travel around a circle in competing directions while players call "bing" or "bong" to track the chaos.

Description

exercises.name: Hands Up
exercises.description:
Stand in a circle with palms raised to shoulder height, facing sideways toward neighbors.

  • One player gently pushes a neighbor's hand to start a wave moving clockwise around the circle
  • When your left hand is pushed, push your right hand; when your right is pushed, push your left
  • The leader can add multiple waves simultaneously, moving in either direction
  • Continue until waves create controlled chaos, emphasizing listening and group focus
exercises.description_raw:
Hands Up

By learnimprov | February 6, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Hands. The Wave.

Introduction

Everyone into a circle. Hold your hands up and have your palms face to the sides

Description

Instead of holding hands, like in the warm up Hands, the players put their palms up over their heads and push on their neighbour’s hand instead of squeezing.

One player in the circle will start by gently pushing the hand of the player on her right. That player will push the hand of the player on her right. So if someone pushes your left hand you push your right hand. This will create a wave of pushes that hurtle around the circle (clockwise). This is the simplest form of Hands Up. It kind of looks like a mini-wave.

If a player has their right hand gently pushed they will gently push their left hand. This will create a wave of pushes to hurtle in the opposite direction of the paragraph above (counter clockwise). So we can have a wave of gentle pushes moving to the right or the left.

The leader can keep adding gentle pushes into the circle. There could be a wave heading to the right, with another waver following behind. There could be a wave going to the right and left at the same time. Usually the circle will keep adding waves until chaos reigns.

This is an ice breaker warm up that focuses on listening with out words and concentration. Since it usually ends in a chaotic pushing of sweaty palms Hands also reminds players that failure is okay, and group failure is even more okay.

None.

Variations

  • Gate Controlled – the player cannot gently push until the gentle push on them has ended. This changes the rate of the push wave moving around the circle.
  • Hands – Players hold each others hands and squeeze instead.

Credits

Picked this up from Improv Encyclopedia

Category: Warm-Up Tags: circle, wave
exercises.summary: Gentle hand pushes ripple around a circle, alternating sides to create synchronized waves moving in multiple directions.

exercises.name: Jump-Association
exercises.description:
Everyone mills around the room. The leader calls out a player's name, who jumps and strikes a pose. All other players rush to surround that player and mirror poses around them, creating a tableau.

  • Called player jumps and holds a random pose
  • Remaining players quickly gather around and create complementary poses
  • Hold the still scene for a few beats
  • Return to milling and repeat with a new player
  • Leader can call out endowments (heavy, light, sideways) to shape the jumping player's pose
exercises.description_raw:
Jump-Association

By learnimprov | February 6, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Jump.

Introduction

Everyone start milling about the room. The warm up will get us working together and finding new ways to move.

Description

Association jump is a physical association warm up. It could also be used as an exercise.

The leader calls out the name of one of the players milling about the room. That player will make a small jump and strike a random pose. The remaining players in the room rush to that player and strike poses around her. The players will create a still scene, not unlike a tableau.

Like it’s variation Jump. The leader can call out endowments for the jumping player. Descriptions like: heavy, small, big, sideways, light, etc. The player the descriptor to inform their position however they like. The remaining players that come to her side are only informed by her pose, not the endowment from the leader.

The players hold the tableau for a few beats and then return to milling. The collection shape created by the group need not be still. For example they could quickly create a machine or an alien.

The leader can add esoteric endowments that make no sense, like economic or philanthropic.

Variations

  • Jump

Credits

Picked this up from Improv Encyclopedia

Category: Warm-Up Tags: characters, ensemble, jumping, milling
exercises.summary: One player jumps into a pose while others rush to mirror and surround them in a living tableau before returning to mill.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:
Introduction

exercises.name: Associations-Rhythmic
exercises.description:
Stand in a circle and establish a rhythm together: slap both thighs, clap hands, snap left fingers, snap right fingers. As you snap your right fingers, pass a free-associated word to the next player, who responds on their right snap with the first word that comes to mind.

  • Keep the rhythm steady throughout the circle
  • Say whatever word comes to mind without judgment or censorship
  • Listen closely to each association and respond immediately on your right snap
  • Let go of trying to be funny or clever—trust your first instinct
exercises.description_raw:
Associations-Rhythmic

By learnimprov | February 6, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Snap Clap, Association Circle

Introduction

This warm-up will help us get into the moment, and help us feel easier about being silly.

Description

Free Association is a very common warm up in improv comedy. A free association is when a player associates meaning to something without judgement, censorship or thought. It is quite hard to do. For example a player may be exposed to the word “dog” they may free associate “cat.”

There are as many configurations for this warm up as there are free associations. The most common is to organize in a circle and have one player free associate on the previous player’s free association. It should sound like a series of disconnected words. “mouse”, “trap”, “crab”, “grumpy”, “goofy”, “mouse.”

In Snap Clap Association there is a rhythm that is followed by the entire circle. However the free association is passed around from player to player. The goal is to keep the rhythm and make the associations continuous. Once the rhythm is established the circle may have to drop the right finger snap so players can hear.

  1. Slap both thighs
  2. Clap hands together
  3. Snap left fingers
  4. Snap right fingers
  5. As right finger is passed the player passes an association to the next player.

There is no correct answer in free association. Some players will repeat associations, or have long pauses. This is a good opportunity to explore why this is happening. It is a teaching moment where the leader can discuss.

  • There is no need to try and be funny.
  • You are in a non-judgmental space.
  • Allow the first thing in your mind to come out.

This warm up helps with listening and commitment. Many players have editors in their head. Learn Improv calls it the headitor. The headitor keeps a player from using the first thought that an offer evokes. Free association should help a player break free form their headitor.

  • Physical offers – have the player freely associate a physical offer
  • Rhyming offers – have the players associate in rhyme
  • Last Letter – have the player associate with a word that starts with the last letter of the cue word
  • Closed Eye – have players close their eyes to enhance listening

Variations

  • Firing line – one player is hit with many words to free associate
  • Pass the Chi – players complete the second half of a word
  • Cross Circle – next player to free associate is randomly chosen
  • Milling – Players mill about the room free associating freely
  • Disassociation – Say the opposite of the cue word
  • Rhymes – Freely associate but using rhymes
  • Last Letter – Associate a new word using the last letter of cue word
  • Gibberish – Use gibberish to help free from headitor

Credits

  • The process of free association is credited to Sigmund Fraud.
  • Picked this variation up from Improv Encyclopedia
Category: Warm-Up Tags: circle, rhythm
exercises.summary: Maintain a four-beat rhythm while passing free-associated words around the circle on each right-hand snap.

exercises.name: Associations-Walk Over
exercises.description:
Players stand in a circle and free-associate words based on a cue. When called, a player walks across the circle toward another player, saying one association per step, and stops when they run out of ideas. The receiving player then becomes the next to walk.

  • Form a circle facing inward
  • Leader gives a cue word to start
  • Called player walks across toward another player, speaking one association per step
  • Player stops when associations run out, then that player receives the next cue word
  • Continue until everyone has walked
exercises.description_raw:
Associations-Walk Over

By learnimprov | February 4, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Free Associations.

Introduction

This warm-up will help us get into the moment, and help us feel easier about being silly.

Description

Free Association is a very common warm up in improv comedy. A free association is when a player associates meaning to something without judgement, censorship or thought. It is quite hard to do. For example a player may be exposed to the word “dog” they may free associate “cat.”

Walk Over Association is a simple variation of free association. The player makes as many associations as possible based on one cue word. The player may take a step for each association, and stops walking when they run out of associations. While having a competition is not the best warm up it is still fun to see how far a player can get in the room.

There is no correct answer in free association. Some players will repeat associations, or have long pauses. This is a good opportunity to explore why this is happening. It is a teaching moment where the leader can discuss.

  • There is no need to try and be funny.
  • You are in a non-judgmental space.
  • Allow the first thing in your mind to come out.

This warm up helps with listening and commitment. Many players have editors in their head. Learn Improv calls it the headitor. The headitor keeps a player from using the first thought that an offer evokes. Free association should help a player break free form their headitor.

  • Physical offers – have the player freely associate a physical offer
  • Rhyming offers – have the players associate in rhyme
  • Last Letter – have the player associate with a word that starts with the last letter of the cue word
  • Choose the next player by tossing a prop across the circle

Variations

  • Firing line – one player is hit with many words to free associate
  • Pass the Chi – players complete the second half of a word
  • Milling – Players mill about the room free associating freely
  • Disassociation – Say the opposite of the cue word
  • Rhymes – Freely associate but using rhymes
  • Last Letter – Associate a new word using the last letter of cue word
  • Gibberish – Use gibberish to help free from headitor

Credits

The process of free association is credited to Sigmund Fraud

Category: Warm-Up Tags: circle, words
exercises.summary: One player walks across a circle speaking associations with each step until ideas run out, then passes the cue to their destination.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:
Introduction

This warm-up will help us get into the moment, and help us feel easier about being silly.

Walk Over Association is a simple variation of free association. The player makes as many associations as possible based on one cue word. The player may take a step for each association, and stops walking when they run out of associations. While having a competition is not the best warm up it is still fun to see how far a player can get in the room.

exercises.name: Associations-Cross Circle
exercises.description:
Players stand in a circle and free-associate words without judgment or filtering. One player says a word, then randomly points to another player in the circle who responds with the first word that comes to mind, creating a chain of unfiltered associations.

  • Form a circle facing inward
  • First player says any word aloud
  • That player points to someone else in the circle
  • The pointed player immediately says their free association and points to another player
  • Continue until the flow feels natural, allowing pauses and repetitions
exercises.description_raw:
Associations-Cross Circle

By learnimprov | February 3, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Free Associations.

Introduction

This warm-up will help us get into the moment, and help us feel easier about being silly.

Description

Free Association is a very common warm up in improv comedy. A free association is when a player associates meaning to something without judgement, censorship or thought. It is quite hard to do. For example a player may be exposed to the word “dog” they may free associate “cat.”

exercises.name: Associations-Pass the Chi
exercises.description:
Players stand in a circle and pass energy around while free-associating words. One player says half a word, and the next player completes it to form a full word, then passes the energy to another player who starts a new half-word.

  • Form a circle facing inward
  • Player one says half a word with elongated sounds, like "mmmooow"
  • Next player completes it: "ssss" makes "mouse"
  • That player passes energy to anyone in the circle and starts a new half-word
  • Continue without judgment or pausing to think
exercises.description_raw:
Associations-Pass the Chi

By learnimprov | February 3, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Free Associations

Introduction

This warm-up will help us get into the moment, and help us feel easier about being silly.

Description

Free Association is a very common warm up in improv comedy. A free association is when a player associates meaning to something without judgement, censorship or thought. It is quite hard to do. For example a player may be exposed to the word “dog” they may free associate “cat.”

Find the Chi is quite different from other free association warm ups. The cue that the player gives is half a word. The next player must complete the word. For example one player will offer “mmmmmooooooowwww” and the next player would finish with “ssss.” This would become “mouse.” Another example could be “terrrr” followed by “app.” To create the word “trap.”

Find the Chi is most commonly organized in a circle, but any other configurations like Firing Line or Cross Circle should work fine.

There is no correct answer in free association. Some players will repeat associations, or have long pauses. This is a good opportunity to explore why this is happening. It is a teaching moment where the leader can discuss.

  • There is no need to try and be funny.
  • You are in a non-judgmental space.
  • Allow the first thing in your mind to come out.

This warm up helps with listening and commitment. Many players have editors in their head. Learn Improv calls it the headitor. The headitor keeps a player from using the first thought that an offer evokes. Free association should help a player break free form their headitor.

  • None

Variations

  • Firing line – one player is hit with many words to free associate
  • Cross Circle – next player to free associate is randomly chosen
  • Milling – Players mill about the room free associating freely
  • Disassociation – Say the opposite of the cue word
  • Rhymes – Freely associate but using rhymes
  • Last Letter – Associate a new word using the last letter of cue word
  • Gibberish – Use gibberish to help free from headitor

Credits

The process of free association is credited to Sigmund Fraud

exercises.summary: Energy passes around a circle as players complete half-words their neighbors start, building full words through collaborative sound-making.

exercises.name: Associations-Firing Line
exercises.description:
One player stands front and center while the rest of the group lines up facing them. The line rapidly calls out words one at a time, and the center player immediately responds with the first word that comes to mind, without pausing or filtering.

  • Center player commits to saying the first association that comes up, no editing
  • Line players have their word ready and fire it out as soon as the center player finishes
  • Keep the pace fast with no pauses between associations
  • Repeat words and disconnected responses are all valid—this breaks the internal editor
exercises.description_raw:
Associations-Firing Line

By learnimprov | February 3, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Free Associations

Introduction

This warm-up will help us get into the moment. One player front and center and rest line up facing her.

Description

Free Association is a very common warm up in improv comedy. A free association is when a player associates meaning to something without judgement, censorship or thought. It is quite hard to do. For example a player may be exposed to the word “dog” they may free associate “cat.”

There are as many configurations for this warm up as there are free associations. Regardless of the configuration a Free Associations should sound like a series of disconnected words. “mouse”, “trap”, “crab”, “grumpy”, “goofy”, “mouse.” The words should spew forth without pauses.

In Firing Line one player is put front and center and the other players line up facing her. The players on the line pepper the front and center player with words to associate with. The players on the firing line should have their word ready to go, and spit them out as soon as the front and center player has finished their association. The players on the line are not free associating.

There is no correct answer in free association. Some players will repeat associations, or have long pauses. This is a good opportunity to explore why this is happening. It is a teaching moment where the leader can discuss.

  • There is no need to try and be funny.
  • You are in a non-judgmental space.
  • Allow the first thing in your mind to come out.

This warm up helps with listening and commitment. Many players have editors in their head. Learn Improv calls it the headitor. The headitor keeps a player from using the first thought that an offer evokes. Free association should help a player break free form their headitor.

  • Physical offers – have the player freely associate a physical offer
  • Rhyming offers – have the players associate in rhyme
  • Last Letter – have the player associate with a word that starts with the last letter of the cue word

Variations

  • Pass the Chi – players complete the second half of a word
  • Cross Circle – next player to free associate is randomly chosen
  • Milling – Players mill about the room free associating freely
  • Disassociation – Say the opposite of the cue word
  • Rhymes – Freely associate but using rhymes
  • Last Letter – Associate a new word using the last letter of cue word
  • Gibberish – Use gibberish to help free from headitor

Credits

The process of free association is credited to Sigmund Fraud

exercises.summary: A center player rapidly fires back word associations as a line of players machine-gun prompts at them without pause or filtering.

exercises.name: Conga Line
exercises.description:
One player leads a moving line across the room with an invented gait or dance style while others follow behind, mimicking only the player directly in front of them.

  1. Form a single line with one player at the front as the leader
  2. Leader creates a repetitive, stylized movement or dance while moving through the space
  3. Each player follows and copies the movements of the person directly ahead, not the leader
  4. Expect delay and decay in the movements as they pass down the line—embrace the imperfection
  5. Leader can reverse direction or flip around to change who leads
exercises.description_raw:
Conga Line

By learnimprov | February 3, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Aerobics, Flock Dance

Introduction

This is a physical warm-up and ice breaker. One player is in front of the line and all players fall in behind her as she moves about the room.

Description

Conga Line is equivalent to Flock Dance. However if workshop space is limited and improvised Conga Line can do the trick. This is different from the official Conga dance in that the lead player creates their own stylized gait and the player behind her follows and so on.

This warm up is best done with music. The first player starts a repetitive movement or dance. The two players immediately behind the lead player mimic her movements as best as possible. Each row of players follows the row of players ahead of them. Flock Dance works best if the players only try and mimic the players in the line ahead of them. There should be both a delay and a decay in the lead player’s movements.

Flock Dance is a physical warm up that also helps players by getting them to move in uncharacteristic ways. Often players will focus only on the lead player even if they are in the last line. It is important to encourage the players to only follow those directly in front of them. Players can use this as a practise for getting things wrong. Trying to do a perfect Flock Dance defeats the purpose of Flock Dance. Embrace failure, embrace chaos, and let go of being “right.”

  • lead player starts to move backwards
  • lead player flips around making the end player now the lead

Variations

  • Aerobics – there is no layering of the players and everyone copies lead
  • Flock Dance – Lead player is the pinnacle of a pyramid

Credits

North America appropriation of African rhythms appropriated by Cuba.

Category: Accepting Listening Problem Warm-Up Tags: characters, circle, failure, line, movement
exercises.summary: One player leads a moving line with invented movements while each follower mimics only the person directly ahead, creating hilarious distortion down the chain.

exercises.name: Alphabet Circle
exercises.description:
Get into a tight circle with heads lowered and eyes closed. The group recites the alphabet one letter at a time, with different players speaking each letter without overlapping.

  1. Everyone stands in a circle, looks down, and closes their eyes
  2. One player starts by saying "A"
  3. A different player says "B," then another says "C," and so on
  4. If two players speak simultaneously, restart at "A"
  5. Continue until someone successfully calls out "Z"
exercises.description_raw:
Alphabet Circle

By learnimprov | January 31, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Shared counting, Countdown, Synergy, No Doubles.

Introduction

This exercise is designed to get us all in synch. Get into a tight circle.

Description

This warm up is exactly the same as Digits, however the circle moves through the alphabet instead of counting.

Everyone in the circle looks down at the ground and closes their eyes. We will try to count to Z as a group. One player starts at the beginning “A.” Then a different player counts off “B.” With heads lowered and eyes closed no one should know who will speak the next number. If two players speak at the same time then the group must start again at “A.” The group can keep trying until they one player calls out “Z.” At this point there is much rejoicing.

None.

Variations

  • Digits – count to 20 instead of the alphabet.
  • Word at a time – If two words come out a the same time story restarts.

Credits

None

Category: Warm-Up Tags: circle, counting, ensemble, literacy, numeracy
exercises.summary: Group members call out alphabet letters in sequence with eyes closed, restarting if two people speak simultaneously.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:
Introduction

Description

One player starts at the beginning “A.” Then a different player counts off “B.” With heads lowered and eyes closed no one should know who will speak the next number. If two players speak at the same time then the group must start again at “A.” The group can keep trying until they one player calls out “Z.” At this point there is much rejoicing.

Variations

  • Digits – count to 20 instead of the alphabet.
  • Word at a time – If two words come out a the same time story restarts.

exercises.name: High Fives
exercises.description:
Players stand in a large circle facing inward. Two players are called simultaneously and walk across the circle toward each other, high-fiving in the middle before taking each other's spots. The two players then each call out two new players to cross, creating continuous movement across the circle.

  • Form a large circle facing inward
  • One player calls out another player's name across the circle and begins walking toward their spot
  • The called player immediately calls out a different player and walks toward their spot
  • When two players cross paths, they high-five in the middle
  • Continue as players call names and move to new spots, maintaining constant crossing motion
exercises.description_raw:
High Fives

By learnimprov | January 31, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Name Game, Shriner Warm-up

Introduction

This warm up will help us all get moving, listening and learning some names. Let’s form a large circle.

Description

High Fives is the same warm up as Cross Circle. The difference is that there are two player names (and hence two players) being called at the same time. As the players cross the circle they high five each other in the middle. After the high five the two players call on two more players and take their place in the circle.

In this warm up players will be crossing the circle to occupy another player’s spot across the circle. They signal who’s spot they are going to occupy by calling our the player’s name. After a player’s name has been called out that player call’s out another players name and moves toward their spot.

If the above explanation worked there will always be a player walking across the circle to a new spot. If the above explanation did not work try the painfully detailed one below.

All the players are arranged in a large circle facing inwards. A player named Eh will randomly pick a player across the circle and call out their name. Let’s presume the player across the circle is named Bee. Player Eh will make eye contact, point towards and then call out “Bee.” Hopefully this will have gotten Bee’s attention.

Player Eh will start to move across the circle towards Bee. Player’s do not run across the circle. Player Eh is going to occupy player Bee’s spot so player Bee has to get moving.

Now player Bee will make eye contact with, point towards and call the name of another player in the circle. Surprisingly this new player’s name is Cee. Bee will start casually moving towards Cee in the circle. Player Eh will now occupy the empty space in the circle left by Bee as she has now started walking towards Cee.

This can continue endlessly until fatigue or hunger sets in

This is a great warm up for learning names. Encourage players to say the names loud and clear so that others can learn names as well. However learning names barely scratches the surface of what this warm up offers. Every aspect of LACE, and more, is demonstrated in cross circle.

Listening. Clearly players must be listening for their names. That is clear enough. There is a lot more to listen for in cross circle. Players can work in listening with their eyes for the initial eye contact. Players can work on listening to the whole stage by learning names of players as others cross the circle. Emotional and subtext listening can be picked up when a player is projecting the angst of not knowing the other player’s name.

Commitment. There is tremendous social pressure to get things right, especially names. Getting players to publicly take risks regarding other players names helps build risk taking behaviour. There is not wrong. Your fellow players will support you. In improv you never know what is next. So stick to your choice loud and proud. If Nancy turns out to be Norma you will be forgiven and we all will learn as a group.

Support. We feel our names are important to us. If someone messes our name up in cross circle we will be supportive. This trains being supportive at all times. Supportive players makes cross circle work better as well.

Focus. Taking it. We deserve the attention of others. If we don’t get it with eye contact we point, and then we call out the player’s name. Focus is also about giving focus. Every player must throw focus to the player

Rules Not Goals. Errors are not corrected they are supported. A successful cross circle is about intent and messing it up is funny. Eventually the group of players will self correct cross circle especially once

Variations

  • Cross Circle
  • Subtle Crossings – remove pointing and player naming rely on just eye contact.
  • Word at a Time – replace the player’s name with a word to create a word at a time story.

Credits

None

Category: Listening Problem Warm-Up Tags: circle, clapping, ice breaker, lace, names, walking
exercises.summary: Two players cross the circle to high-five in the middle, then call new names to keep the motion flowing continuously.

There is not wrong. Your fellow players will support you. In improv you never know what is next. So stick to your choice loud and proud. If Nancy turns out to be Norma you will be forgiven and we all will learn as a group.

Support. We feel our names are important to us. If someone messes our name up in cross circle we will be supportive. This trains being supportive at all times. Supportive players makes cross circle work better as well.

Focus. Taking it. We deserve the attention of others. If we don’t get it with eye contact we point, and then we call out the player’s name. Focus is also about giving focus. Every player must throw focus to the player

Rules Not Goals. Errors are not corrected they are supported. A successful cross circle is about intent and messing it up is funny. Eventually the group of players will self correct cross circle especially once

Variations

  • Cross Circle
  • Subtle Crossings – remove pointing and player naming rely on just eye contact.
  • Word at a Time – replace the player’s name with a word to create a word at a time story.

exercises.name: Receiver Right Clap
exercises.description:
Players stand in a large circle and pass energy across the room using sound and gesture.

  • One player makes eye contact with someone across the circle, lunges forward with a "whoosh" sound and pointing gesture
  • The selected player accepts with a sucking sound and catching gesture
  • The player immediately to the right of the catcher sends the next whoosh to anyone in the circle
  • Continue rapid exchanges of whooshes and catches around the circle
  • Increase difficulty by sending to the left or to players several spots away
exercises.description_raw:
Receiver Right Clap

By learnimprov | January 30, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Fart Circle.

Introduction

This warm up is to get players listening, responding and reacting without thinking. Everyone gets into a large circle.

Description

One player in the circle chooses a random player across from them in the circle. They signal the selected player by making eye contact, lunging into the circle and gesturing towards them with an accompanying “whoosh” sound. It should be very obvious who is has been given the whoosh. The selected player accepts the whoosh by making a sucking sound accompanied by a catching gesture. The player to the immediate right of the player that just made the catch and the sucking sound sends out a new whoosh, and so on. This continues whooshes and slurps are dancing across the circle.

Ideally there will be continuous whooshing and slurping. Once the players get the hang of it the leader can raise the challenge by switching to the immediate left side of the catching player or some arbitrary number of players away.

This warm up is essentially a variation of clap focus, but it required a unique explanation that a new warm up was added. Note I did not create yet another warm up called Receiver Left Clap. Learn Improv keeping it classy.

This warm up helps players develop taking focus, and the immediate rewards of making eye contact to connect with other players. On a rudimentary level this is a listening exercise as well.

  • Make rude noises instead whooshing. For example I have heard this done with adult movie noises.

Variations

  • Snap Focus – Same warm up but snapping fingers instead of clapping.
  • Fart Circle – make sophomoric simulated gastronomic explosive noises across the circle

Credits

Camp camp summer cramp.

Category: Listening Problem Warm-Up Tags: circle, clapping, listening, reactions
exercises.summary: Energy whooshes across a circle with sound and gesture, always passing to the right catcher's neighbor.

exercises.name: Zombie Walk
exercises.description:
A trust-building warm-up where blindfolded players mill slowly around the room while spotters on the walls ensure safety. One player secretly becomes a zombie and spreads the apocalypse by grabbing wrists.

  1. Assign spotters to stand on each wall. All other players mill slowly around the room with eyes closed.
  2. Select one player as patient zero. When the zombie nudges another player, grab their wrist. That player calls out "Brrrrraaaaaiiinz" and becomes a zombie.
  3. New zombies mill about with eyes closed, seeking brains. When two zombies meet, both call out "huuuuuuuuunnnnngry" and continue.
  4. Spotters gently redirect wandering players back to center by touching shoulders. Spotters can make zombie noises as distractions.
  5. Exercise ends when the zombie apocalypse is complete.
exercises.description_raw:
Zombie Walk

By learnimprov | January 30, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Screamers, Raccoon City.

Introduction

This warm up builds trust amongst players. We need safety players on each wall. Everyone else start slowly milling around the room.

Description

The players on the outside are spotters. Ideally there will be more than four spotters. Remember safety first. Before starting fully explain this warm up to the players. Make it clear that they will feel vulnerable with their eyes closed and they will be unexpectedly touched without explicit consent. The leader must ensure that players feel safe to not participate. No player needs to explain why they choose not to participate in Zombie Walk (or anything else for that matter).

Instruct the players milling about to close their eyes and continue slowly milling around. It is important to emphasize to move slowly. Advise them that they will be touched in one of three ways. First they will be slowly bumping into each other. They all should be moving slowing enough that this will be safe. The next most likely touch they will receive will be from the spotters on stationed outside the players milling around. This touch will involve the spotters gently redirecting players back into the center of the room by touching their shoulders. The last touch is the most dramatic. If the another player grabs your wrist you have been bitten by the zombie. You will let out an overly dramatic cry of “Brrrrraaaaaiiinz.”

The leader will select one player to be patient zero of the zombie apocalypse. The zombie is milling around with all the other players. However when the zombie nudges into another player she will grab her wrist indicating that the zombie has feasted on brains. As described above the player who get’s her wrist grabbed will mournfully call out “brains.” That player is now a zombie and she will mill about with her eyes closed looking for brains as well. If two zombies meet each other they will mournfully call out “huuuuuuuuunnnnngry” and continue looking for brains. The warm up ends when the zombie apocalypse in complete.

  • The spotters can make zombie noises as a distraction.
  • don’t pick a zombie and let them wander for hours.

Variations

Vampire – Instead of the confidant being a zombie she is a vampire.

Credits

Philanthropic Division of the Umbrella Coroporation

Category: Problem Teamwork Warm-Up Tags: ensemble, safety, trust
exercises.summary: One blindfolded player spreads zombie infection by grabbing wrists while others mill safely with spotters redirecting strays.

exercises.name: Snap Focus
exercises.description:
Players stand in a large circle and pass focus by making eye contact, lunging forward, and clapping sharply at another player across the circle. The selected player accepts and claps at a different player, creating a continuous rhythm of claps flying back and forth.

  1. Form a large circle facing inward
  2. One player makes eye contact with someone across the circle, lunges in, and claps at them clearly
  3. That player accepts and repeats with a new player
  4. Continue building speed until claps sound rapid and constant
  5. Optional: players not clapping snap fingers to maintain energy
exercises.description_raw:
Snap Focus

By learnimprov | January 30, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Clap Focus. Pass Clap.

Introduction

This warm up is to get players listening, responding and reacting without thinking. Everyone gets into a large circle.

Description

One player in the circle chooses a random player across from them in the circle. They signal the selected player by making eye contact, lunging into the circle and clapping “at them.” It should be really obvious who is has been given the clap. The selected player accepts the offer of the clap and repeats the process with a different player in the circle. This process continues until there are claps flying back and forth across the circle.

Ideally there will be a constant clapping noise. Eventually the players will be so attentive and so quick to respond it sounds like one person clapping rapidly. This warm-up usually ends when people’s hand are denuded of flesh.

One could insert literally any gesture in place of clapping. I have seen nods, toe flicks, middle fingers, hellos, salutes, etc. The two most common versions of this warm up are clap focus and snap focus.

This warm up helps players develop taking focus, and the immediate rewards of making eye contact to connect with other players. On a rudimentary level this is a listening exercise as well.

  • Distractions – those players that are not clapping are snapping their fingers.
  • No Clap – the players do not clap but merely point across the circle to the other player. To make the the warm up a bit more challenging remove any physical cues and move back and forth across the circles with only eye contact.
  • Telepathy – Eyes are closed and players signal each other using their inherent psionic power.

Variations

Clap Focus – Same warm up but snapping fingers instead of clapping.

Credits

Camp camp summer cramp.

Category: Listening Problem Warm-Up Tags: circle, listening, reactions, snapping
exercises.summary: Eye contact and sharp claps ricochet across a circle, building rhythmic momentum as players accept and redirect focus at accelerating speed.
