exercises.name: Bippity Bibbity Bop
exercises.description:
Everyone stands in a circle with one player in the center. The center player spins, then suddenly points at someone while yelling "bippity bippity bop" as fast as possible. The pointed player must say "bop" before the center player finishes. If they're too slow or say it when the center player yells just "bop" instead, they swap into the center.

  1. Form a circle with one player in the middle
  2. Center player spins and points at a circle player while rapidly yelling "bippity bippity bop"
  3. Pointed player must respond "bop" before the center player finishes the phrase
  4. If too slow or fooled by a fake "bop," they become the new center player
exercises.description_raw:
Bippity Bibbity Bop

By learnimprov | January 30, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Bippity Bop, Bappety Boo, Boom bing Buckety Wing Fing Fatah.

Introduction

Everyone form a circle with one player in the centre. This warm-up breaks the ice, gets people stressed and prepares one for failure.

Description

The player in the centre of the circle starts to spin madly. They may close their eyes, they may count to ten, they may be silent. The goal of the player in the center is to “surprise” one of the players in the circle. The player in the center must make a strong and clear choice about which player in the circle has been “surprised.”

The player in the center will yell “bippity bippity bop” as fast as possible. If the “surprised” player in the circle does not say “bop” before the player in the center yells “bippity bippity bop” the surprised player goes into the center of the circle.

There is other linguistic trickery that can allow the player in the center to escape. If the player in the center yells “bop.” The “surprised” player must remain stoically silent. If they say “bop” they have been made a fool of and now must head into the center of the circle of shame.

While I have been poking fun at this warm up it can be useful for warming up both commitment and listening. Players must be listening to the cue of being pointed at. Folks who overthink and worry about how they appear can get some help warming up their commitment.

Bippity Bippity Bop is a name with two structures. Let your house decide which you will use, but be aware the there is Bippity Bippity Bop and then there is Bippity Bippity Bop.

Gimmicks

Give the player in the center a really long phrase other than Bippity Bippity Bop to say.

Variations

  • Bippity Bippity BunnBop – Instead of yelling “bippity bippity bop” the player in the center “surprises” a player who becomes the centerpiece of a 3 part bunny character.
  • Bippity Bippity Bopaphant – Instead of yelling “bippity bippity bop” the player in the center “surprises” a player who becomes the centerpiece of a 3 part elephant character.

Credits

Summer camps throughout the ages. I also took the liberty of renaming the elephant and bunny subs of Bippity Bippity Bop.

Category: Listening Warm-Up Tags: characters, circle, commitment, ice breaker, listening
alternativeNames: Boom bing Buckety Wing Fing Fatah.
exercises.summary: A center player spins and points while rapid-firing "bippity bippity bop," and the target must respond "bop" before the phrase ends or swap places.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:
Introduction

Everyone form a circle with one player in the centre. This warm-up breaks the ice, gets people stressed and prepares one for failure.

Description

The player in the centre of the circle starts to spin madly. They may close their eyes, they may count to ten, they may be silent. The goal of the player in the center is to “surprise” one of the players in the circle. The player in the center must make a strong and clear choice about which player in the circle has been “surprised.”

Gimmicks

Variations

  • Bippity Bippity BunnBop – Instead of yelling “bippity bippity bop” the player in the center “surprises” a player who becomes the centerpiece of a 3 part bunny character.
  • Bippity Bippity Bopaphant – Instead of yelling “bippity bippity bop” the player in the center “surprises” a player who becomes the centerpiece of a 3 part elephant character.

exercises.name: Bippity Bibbity BunnBop
exercises.description:
Everyone forms a circle with one player spinning in the center. The center player points at someone to "surprise" them, then yells "bippity bippity bunnybop" as fast as possible.

  1. The pointed-at player forms the bunny's buck teeth while the two players on either side mime tall floppy ears—all three must chew carrot sounds together.
  2. The three-part bunny must be fully formed before the center player finishes yelling.
  3. If any part is missing, slow, or overdone, that player replaces the center player.
  4. The center player decides immediately if unclear who failed.
  5. Continue until the group decides to stop.
exercises.description_raw:
Bippity Bibbity BunnBop

By learnimprov | January 30, 2019
0 Comment

Synonyms

Elephant, Bunny, Bippity Bippity Bop

Introduction

Everyone form a circle with one player in the centre. This warm-up breaks the ice, gets people listening and gets the blood flowing.

Description

The player in the centre of the circle starts to spin madly. They may close their eyes, they may count to ten, they may be silent. The goal of the player in the center is to “surprise” one of the players in the circle. The player in the center must make a strong and clear choice about which player in the circle has been “surprised.”

Three players in the circle must quickly form a three part bunny character. The player pointed at must mime the buck teeth, and the two players on either side of the trunk mime tall floppy ears. All three must make a carrot chewing noise like a happy bunny. This is the simplest description of the bunny. There will be many house variations on how to create the 3 part bunny.

The challenge is to create the 3 part bunny character very quickly. There is not room for indecision. The player in the center of the circle is going to be yelling “bippity bippity bunnybop” as fast as possible. If the 3 part bunny character is not munching a carrot (fully created) before the player in the center finishes yelling “bippity bippity bunnybop” someone from the bunny character will be moved to the center.

Typically some part of the bunny will be slow to react. It may be missing a right or left ear, it’s trunk may be flaccid, or someone forgets to trumpet. Whichever player is responsible for this travesty of character development replaces the player in the circle. The circle of life is continued until someone vomits.

Players that jump the gun and create extra ears, or extra teeth or some other mutated bunny appendage also replace the player in the center of the circle. If for some reason there is a tie or it is unclear which player messed up the 3 part bunny character the player in the center decides immediately who replaces her in the center of the circle. If the player in the center is too slow to call out their replacement they will stay in the center.

This is a great listening and commitment warm up. Players must be listening to the cue of being pointed at, as well as listening for when their neighbour has been “surprised.” It is definitely an example of listening with one’s eyes and brain instead of the usual listening for words with your ears. Players must respond so fast to build their 3 part bunny character that there is no room for hesitation. Folks who overthink and worry about how they appear can get some relief with this warm up.

Bippity Bippity Bop is a name with two structures. Let your house decide which you will use, but be aware the there is Bippity Bippity Bop and then there is Bippity Bippity Bop.

Gimmicks

Move from an bunny to a more complicated multi part character. For example a dragon with flaming mouth, two wings and a long tail. Making the character asymmetric heightens the level of listening and attention to detail required.

Make up your own house characters: Charlies Angels, Church, Car, Tank, Snake, etc.

Variations

  • Bippity Bippity Bopaphant- Players create a chewing bunny with two tall ears and make a carrot chewing noise. While these variations are endless bunny is very common.
  • Bippity Bippity Bop – No characters involved at all. Just fast talking deceit.

Credits

Summer camps throughout the ages. I also took the liberty of renaming the elephant and bunny subs of Bippity Bippity Bop.

Category: Listening Warm-Up Tags: characters, circle, commitment, ice breaker, listening

exercises.name: Mirrors
exercises.description:
Stand in pairs of similar height facing each other. Copy each other's movements simultaneously as if you were mirrors, with one player leading while the leader designates who starts. Focus on supporting your partner with smooth, subtle movements rather than trying to trick them.

  • Begin with the designated leader moving slowly while their partner mirrors exactly
  • Switch leadership when the leader signals, keeping the mirroring seamless
  • Gradually transition to independent movement where no one can tell who is leading
  • Pairs can combine into groups of four, moving toward each other while maintaining mirror synchronization
  • Groups can form circles and add harmonic sounds matching the collective movement
exercises.description_raw:
Mirrors

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

None

Introduction

Please get into pairs that are roughly the same height.

Description

Mirrors is both an exercise and a warm up.

Each of the players should copy each other’s movements as if they were a mirror of each other. If one player moves the other player will copy that move and vice versa. Initially the leader will select one the two players to start moving, and switch between the two mirrored players. Eventually the players will be independently moving and being mirrored. In an ideal situation no one should be able to tell that either player is leading.

The goal of this Mirrors is to support your mirrored player. There is a strong desire to put the other player at risk or try and mess them up. This is a good opportunity to encourage players to be supportive and focus on listening and subtly taking focus. It is also common for mirrored players to move fast. Remind them that the exercise is neither a race or chance to fool players.

There are many complications that the leader can add to this exercise. Task to mirrors to join into a four way mirror. Encourage the pairs of players to find a way to move towards each other but to maintain mirroring. The clump of four players should slowly conform into a four way mirror.

The leader can keep combining mirrors to create interesting patterns that appear as circles all moving ins synch. Once the groups have become circles the leader can task them with finding harmonics or any repetitive noise that goes with their group movement.

Gimmicks

  • None.

Variations

  • Mirroring Scenes – A group of players mirror a ongoing scene.
  • Distorting Mirror – One of the pair intentionally distorts the movements like a carnival mirror.

Credits

Grecian Society for the Control of Medusae.

Category: Exercise Less Problem Warm-Up Tags: creepy, ensemble, reflections
exercises.summary: One player leads slow movements while their partner mirrors exactly, then seamlessly switch roles until leadership becomes invisible.

Description

  • Distorting Mirror – One of the pair intentionally distorts the movements like a carnival mirror.

exercises.name: Freeze Tag-Exercise
exercises.description:
Two volunteers start a physical scene center stage while the rest form a line. The leader calls freeze at an unexpected moment, and a line player tags one of the frozen players, takes their exact physical pose, and initiates a completely new scene from that position. The tagged player goes to the back of the line and the process repeats.

  1. Two players begin a scene with strong physical action in the center while others watch from a line
  2. Leader calls freeze; a line player immediately tags one frozen player and assumes their exact pose
  3. Tagged player exits to back of line; new player starts a fresh scene from the inherited pose
  4. Continue indefinitely, encouraging commitment to the first idea that fits the physical position
exercises.description_raw:
Freeze Tag-Exercise

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

Clap Out. Freeze.

Introduction

Please form a line with two volunteers front and center.

Description

After the pithy aphorism of Yes And, there is no greater staple of the improv teaching than Freeze Tag.

The leader encourages the two front and center players to begin a scene which involves some level of physical activity. Some of the classic ones are two characters fishing, two characters working on some chore. While there will be dialogue between the characters Freeze Tag depends on physical action.

At some unexpected moment the leader will call out freeze and the two players will instantly freeze in place. A player from the line will immediately approach either of the two players and tag them. The tagged player goes to the back of the line. The new player assumes the exact physical pose that the player they tagged was holding. The new player is tasked with initiating a completely new scene that reflects her acquired pose. This process can continue indefinitely.

By encouraging the players to assume the exact pose of the player tagged out this exercise can help improve their listening skills. There is an element of spontaneity encouraged as often players will find themselves in poses they could not have imagined themselves in.

The leader should watch for players changing their pose to reflect a preconceived idea. It is an opportunity to support spontaneity reminding the players that they need not judges their ideas and they will be supported on stage.

There are many default scenes that allow a player to justify any pose that they find themselves. Experienced players should be discouraged from from employing these standards. Implore them to take risks and commit to random ideas instead of safety.

  • Fighting
  • Dancing
  • Adhesive accidents
  • A model being painted
  • Store mannequins
  • Psychotic episodes
  • Dream episodes

Freeze Tag is often treated like a throw away exercise to fill space or end a workshop. This exercise is also used as a performance handle as an audience or player warm up. When Freeze Tag is played with attention to detail every aspect of improv comedy can be reviewed.

Players must be listening to the pose and words of the preceding scene. The must accept the offer of the pose they are striking. They must commit to their first idea with decisiveness. The offer must be expanded upon, and this offer is often a physical one. Freeze Tag offers lots of teaching opportunities as many LACE elements are missed or demonstrated with this exercise.

Freeze Tag is often and excuse to dispense with narrative elements. In an exercise that feels hurried the players, and audience, will still benefit from Settings, Ties, Explorations, and Propels. The scene is usually frozen before it gets to Sorting out.

Gimmicks

  • Don’t call freeze and let the players run through a scene.
  • Rapidly call freeze before players can change the scene.
  • Hold players in a compromising position by not calling freeze.

Variations

  • Endowment Freeze – Each freeze is endowed with a location, emotion or genre. May actually be easier than standard Freeze Tag for some.
  • Structured Freeze – Player must always pick the stage left pose.
  • Eyes Closed Freeze – The next player in faces away from the scene or closes their eyes to they have even less information about the pose.
  • Audience Freeze – Audience calls the Freeze.
  • Space Jump – Specialized Freeze structure where performer are not replaced but increase in number.
  • Freeze For All – When freeze is called any number of players can jump on stage replacing any number of players.

Credits

  • None
Category: Exercise Warm-Up
exercises.summary: Freeze a physical scene, tag in a new player who inherits the pose and launches a fresh scene from that position.

exercises.name: Whoosh
exercises.description:
Players stand in a circle and pass a 'whoosh' signal around by throwing both arms in one direction and saying 'whoosh'. Build complexity by adding reversals with 'wow' (cross arms to change direction), 'whap' (point across circle to skip players), 'groovalicious' (all lean in with groovy gestures), and 'drama lama' (chaotic run and reposition).

  1. Form a circle and establish the basic whoosh passing left or right with arm gestures
  2. Add 'wow' reversals where receiving players cross arms to change direction
  3. Introduce 'whap' to throw focus across the circle with dramatic pointing
  4. Layer in 'groovalicious' (all groove together) and 'drama lama' (chaotic repositioning) before resuming
  5. Vary the words each round to keep it fresh and challenging
exercises.description_raw:
Whoosh

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

Lots and lots.

Introduction

This warm-up gets our brains acting without thinking. Please create a circle.

Description

In this warm-up one of the players throws both arms to their left or right and says ‘whoosh’. That player turns to the next player in the circle, and uses both arms to to pass on a ‘whoosh’. Thus the ‘whoosh’ whooshes around the circle in one direction. It sounds like a continuous whoosh-whoosh-whoosh with players pointing in the direction of the “whoosh.”

Once whoosh is well established then next level of the warm up is introduced. Instead of continuing the whoosh around the circle a player may change the direction of the whoosh. This is indicated by the receiving player saying ‘wow’ and crossing their arms. When met with a wow the whoosh changes direction. The warm up now sounds like whoosh-whoosh-whoosh-whoosh-whoosh-whoosh-wow-whoosh (opposite direction)-whoosh-whoosh. Whooshes sound the same in both directions.

Once bidirectional whooshing has been established the next level of the warm up is introduced. At any time a player can call out “whap” instead of whoosh or wow. Whap allows the player to throw the focus across the circle. The Whap is accompanied with an overly dramatic pointing action. The player that was whapped may whoosh in either direction.

One whooshing, wowing and whapping are established it is time to introduce the next level – groovalicious. When the player calls out Groovalicious all the players lean into the circle and make their grooviest hand and upper body actions. Anyone can pick up the whoosh once these gesticulations are completed.

But wait there is more! The next level of Whoosh to introduce is Drama Lama. When a player calls out Drama Lama all the players chaotically and histrionically run around screaming and change position in the circle.
Anyone can pick up the whoosh once the choas is completed.

Whoosh is fun and requires listening, and focus efforts. Failure is expected, supported and laughed with.

Gimmicks

  • Don’t stop adding ridiculous new actions until failure is inevitable, supported and laughed with.
  • change the words from whoosh, wow and whap to other random words. makes it new every time.

Variations

  • Simulclap
  • Zip Zap Zoop
  • Cross Circle

Credits

None.

Category: Focus Listening Warm-Up Tags: attention, circle, focus
exercises.summary: A circle passes energy with arm gestures and sound effects that reverse direction, skip players, and trigger group movements.

exercises.name: Yes Let’s
exercises.description:
Everyone mills around the room while one person calls out a mime activity for the group to perform together.

  • When an activity is suggested, everyone shouts "Yes let's!" and immediately starts miming it with sound effects
  • Continue the mime for several seconds while moving around the room
  • Someone calls out a new activity and the group responds with "Yes let's!" and switches to the new mime
  • Keep cycling through different activities, accepting each offer enthusiastically
exercises.description_raw:
Yes Let’s

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

None

Introduction

This warm-up focuses on accepting offers and getting silly. Start milling about the room.

Description

Someone will loudly suggest an activity for all the group to mime. Everyone simultaneously yells ‘yes lets’, and starts the do the activity. Once the activity has been mimed for a while new mime activity is yelled out and the group responds with ‘yes lets’ and starts the activity. For example, if someone calls out let’s all dig holes. The group responds with “yes let’s dig holes”, and every manner of hole digging mime will begin (complete with sound effects). The someone will suggest a new activity and the cycle continues.

Yes Let’s is an obvious support warm up. It also helps folks develop rapid mime skills for mime activities that they may not otherwise think of.

Gimmicks

Someone yells out ‘let’s finish this exercise’, or ‘let’s all sit down.’

Variations

Hunting the Whatsit.

Credits

None

Category: Accepting Mime Problem Propel Warm-Up Tags: milling, mime, silly
exercises.summary: Everyone mills around accepting mime activities called out by rotating leaders, responding with enthusiastic "Yes let's!" before performing each one together.

exercises.name: Stretching
exercises.description:
Stand in a circle and gently stretch together, with each player leading one stretch for the group to follow.

  • Leader demonstrates a safe stretch targeting one body part; group mirrors for about one minute
  • Each player takes a turn leading a different stretch, focusing on varied body areas
  • Players may skip any stretch that doesn't feel safe for them—no explanation needed
  • Optional: while stretching, share an improv goal or something positive from your week
exercises.description_raw:
Stretching

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

None

Introduction

Let’s all get limbered up by doing some simple stretching exercises.

Description

The leader should know a bit about stretching and be able to ensure that any stretch done is a safe one. The leader must also make it clear that a player should only participate in stretches that are safe for them. The leader must ensure that players feel safe to not participate. No player needs to explain why they choose not to participate in a stretch (or anything else for that matter).

The warm up seems to work best in a circle, but that is not necessary. Usually the leader starts with a stretch and group follows. Each player contributes a simple stretch that all the other players follow. This warm up works best if different body parts are gently stretched. For example, eight different finger stretches may not be best.

Each stretch lasts about a minute. This is a great time for players to offer some insight to their take on improv comedy. Learn Improv often will ask a player to chat about an improv goal (Listening, Commitment, etc) as they lead the stretch. Another topic is “something good that happened this week.” Stretching in silence is fine.

Gimmicks

Nose stretches, hair stretches, scrotal stretches.

Variations

None.

Credits

Many ancient religions.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: circle, stretch
exercises.summary: Each player leads the circle through a different stretch while the group mirrors, building warmth and focus together.

exercises.name: Song Circle
exercises.description:
Players stand in a circle while one person jumps to the center and sings a song matching a chosen theme. When the singer struggles, another player quickly jumps in to support them with a new song, and the first player steps out. Keep rotating until songs repeat or energy fades.

  1. Form a circle and select a theme like transportation or love
  2. First player jumps to center and sings a related song
  3. When they get stuck, another player jumps in with a new song and the previous singer exits
  4. Continue rotating, supporting each singer by jumping in promptly
  5. If someone is really rocking it, everyone can join in singing
exercises.description_raw:
Song Circle

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

Hot Spot.

Introduction

This warm-up is good for the lungs and gets us taking personal risks. Everyone gather in a circle.

Description

A very general theme is chosen, like transportation or love. Once the theme is chosen players jump into the centre of the circle and start to sing any song related to the theme. Once the player gets stuck on the song, another player must show support and jump into the circle to help with a new song. The previous person in the centre steps out. This continues until songs start to get repeated, or things collapse utterly. The point of the warm-up is not to embarrass people in the centre, but to support the player in the centre. This is done by quickly jumping into the circle once they start to have any trouble at all.

Gimmicks

If a player is rocking it everyone joins.

Variations

Gibberish – allow players to sing in gibberish.

Credits

Camp Puddlestone for the Emotionally Destitute

Category: support Warm-Up Tags: circle, sing, support
exercises.summary: One singer performs a themed song in the center while others jump in to replace them when they falter.

exercises.name: Slow Motion Swordplay
exercises.description:
Mill around the room in slow motion, each player with an imaginary poisonous blade on their forearm. Touch other players with your blade to poison them.

  1. Start milling about the room comfortably
  2. Shift into slow motion as a group
  3. Endow your forearms with poisonous blades
  4. Attempt to touch others while maintaining slow motion throughout
  5. When touched, die dramatically in slow motion and rest on the floor
exercises.description_raw:
Slow Motion Swordplay

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

Poison Arms.

Introduction

This warm-up will get us feeling silly, get the blood flowing, and help get us working together. Start milling about the room.

Description

Once everyone is milling about comfortably the players now start to move in slow motion. Next all the players are endowed with poisonous blades built into their fore arms. The object of this warm up is to touch other players with the poison edge of their sword-arms. The challenge is that players must must must maintain slow motion.

If anyone is touched by the fore arm of another player they will suffer the effect of the poison, also in slow motion. Historically the poison has been lethal and the poisoned players slowly died an over dramatic death and come to rest on the floor. However the poison could have any affect the leader decides: sleeping poison, laughing poison, become a plumber poison. Regardless of the effect of the poison the player ends up motionless on the floor. The warm up ends when there is a large pile of bodies on the floor.

This warm up is not a competition. The urge to win Slow Motion Swordplay is hard to overcome. The warm up gets players accepting their fate and support the group by allowing others to poison them.

Gimmicks

None

Variations

  • Divide the players into teams working to eliminate each other

Credits

None.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: milling, poison, swords
exercises.summary: Milling players move in slow motion with poisoned blades on their forearms, dramatically dying when touched.

exercises.name: Simulclap
exercises.description:
Stand in a circle and work together to pass synchronized claps around the ring.

  1. One player claps while facing another player in the circle, who mirrors the clap simultaneously using eye contact and exaggerated telegraphing.
  2. The player who received the clap turns to face the next player in the circle and repeats the synchronized clap.
  3. Continue passing the clap around the circle, maintaining rhythm and focus.
  4. Once smooth, players can pass the clap back to the previous player to reverse direction, creating a clap that zips around the circle changing directions.
  5. When ready, add a second clap or try variations like snapping, slapping ankles, or saying "yes" instead of clapping.
exercises.description_raw:
Simulclap

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

None.

Introduction

Get into a circle for a listening and focus warm-up.

Description

One player starts off by clapping their hands (a singular clap), while facing another player. The player mimics the the starting player and the goal is to clap simultaneously. The player that received the applause then turns and claps simultaneously with the next player in the circle. This process is continued around the circle in a series of individual claps built by pairs of players.

This is not a competitive warm up. The players must work with each other by making eye contact with their clap partner and telegraphing an exaggerated clapping motion.

Once the simultaneous clapping is moving nicely around the circle the warm up is ready for the next level. A player may now opt to give the clap right back to the player they got it from. This is accomplished with good eye contact and telegraphing gestures. This will reverse the direction of the clapping.

When this is working well there is a clapping sound zipping around the circle occasionally changing direction.

Once the circle is passing the clap around and changing direction in a seamless manner the leader can add in a second clap, or some other kind of repetitive action listed under variations.

Simulclap is a good warm up for enhancing listening and taking focus. Simulclap also allows the group to work with failing in comfort and that there are no rules only goals. Group support can also be demonstrated by the group not losing their rhythm or pointing out missteps.

Gimmicks

Make all kinds of jokes about passing the clap, or getting the clap.

Variations

  • Clap Story – Instead of clapping the player contributes a word from a Word at a Time story. This is really difficult.
  • Lederhosen – Players simultaneously slap their ankles with their hands passing around this dance step.
  • Simulsnap – Snap fingers instead of clapping.
  • Silentclap – Make the gesture of a clap but keep the meat from connecting so no clap sound is created.
  • Pass Yes – Players say yes instead of clapping.

Credits

None

Category: Focus Listening Problem Warm-Up Tags: circle, clapping, dance, snap, Teutonic
exercises.summary: Synchronized claps travel around a circle using eye contact and mirroring, then reverse direction and layer in variations.

exercises.name: Redirection
exercises.description:
A trust-building exercise where one blindfolded player walks slowly in a circle while silent spotters gently redirect them back toward center using light touches on the shoulders.

  1. Form a large circle with spotters standing around the perimeter
  2. One player enters the center, closes their eyes, and walks slowly in any direction
  3. Spotters silently redirect the walker with one or two gentle finger touches on the shoulders when they approach the circle edge
  4. Keep redirections subtle and silent to maintain the walker's vulnerability and trust
  5. Optional: Have the walker make sounds or have spotters shift positions to increase difficulty
exercises.description_raw:
Redirection

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

Walkies.

Introduction

This warm-up helps to build trust in the group members. Everyone get into a large circle.

Description

The players in the circle are spotters. Ideally there will be more than four spotters. Remember safety first. Before starting fully explain this warm up to the players. Make it clear that they will feel vulnerable with their eyes closed and they will be unexpectedly touched without explicit consent. The leader must ensure that players feel safe to not participate. No player needs to explain why they choose not to participate in Redirection (or anything else for that matter).

A player is chosen to get into the middle of the circle and close their eyes. The player then slowly walks in any direction. The spotters in the circle gently redirect the walker as she comes to the edge of the circle. The spotters in the circle need to remain silent so as not to alert the walker when she is about to be turned.

The leader must take time to explain how the walkers are going to be redirected. The walker is redirected by the player in the circle placing one or two fingers on the shoulders of the walker and gently redirecting her back towards the center.

This warm up can be taken to a more advanced level by having the walker make noises distracting her from sensing the spotter circle.

Gimmicks

Have the circle move around the player so that she cannot learn its dimensions.

Variations

None

Credits

None

Category: Accepting Listening Problem Teamwork Warm-Up Tags: circle, trust
exercises.summary: Blindfolded walkers navigate a circle while silent spotters gently redirect them back to center through subtle shoulder touches.

This warm up can be taken to a more advanced level by having the walker make noises distracting her from sensing the spotter circle.

Gimmicks

Have the circle move around the player so that she cannot learn its dimensions.

exercises.name: Popcorn
exercises.description:
Stand in a circle and randomly jump into the air, clapping your hands at unpredictable moments. If your clap happens simultaneously with another player's clap, you're out and step back to watch. After a few rounds, rejoin the circle and keep playing.

  • Everyone forms a tight circle
  • Jump and clap at random, unexpected times
  • Listen carefully for other claps happening at the same moment
  • If two claps sync, both players step out briefly then return
  • Keep the energy high and unpredictable
exercises.description_raw:
Popcorn

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

Jumpers

Introduction

Everyone into a circle for a silly warm-up to get the blood flowing.

Description

Players randomly and unexpectedly jump up in to the air. At some time in the air they clap their hands. Since other players are also randomly jumping and clapping simultaneously the name popcorn seems appropriate. If two players clap their hands at the same time they are out of the circle. This warm up is not competitive and players return to the circle after a few claps.

Yelling the word pop instead of clapping is a very common variation.

The warm up Popcorn gets people moving and helps practise listening for offers other than words (in this case clapping).

Gimmicks

None.

Variations

  • Pop Uranus – Player kernels that are out of the circle create a new a new outer circle, like a planetary ring. Once the inner circle has evaporated players then move outwards to create another ring. If there are lots of players then multiple rings can be made.
  • Clap Out – No jumping. Just clapping or yelling pop!

Credits

Orville R.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: circle, clapping, jumping
exercises.summary: Random jumping and clapping in a circle eliminates players whose claps sync with others.

Description

Variations

  • Clap Out – No jumping. Just clapping or yelling pop!

exercises.name: Pass Catch
exercises.description:
Players stand in a circle with enough space to move arms freely. One player creates an unconventional pose with a matching sound and offers it to their right neighbor, who mirrors it exactly before immediately creating a new pose and sound to pass along. Keep the energy flowing without pausing to think between poses.

  1. Form a circle with adequate arm space between players
  2. Player one strikes a silly pose with a corresponding sound
  3. Right neighbor mirrors the pose and sound precisely
  4. That player instantly creates a new pose and sound to pass right
  5. Continue around the circle without stopping or hesitation
exercises.description_raw:
Pass Catch

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

Sound Circle. Accepting Circle.

Introduction

This warm up will help us get the body warmed up and get more comfortable about being silly. Please organize in to a circle.

Description

Ensure that the players have enough space to move their arms freely without clocking their neighbour in the head.

A player will throw strike an unconventional pose and create a corresponding sound to go along with the pose. The pose and noise are given as an offer to the player on her right. That player accepts the offer by reflecting the pose and noise as closely as possible. After recreating that pose and noise the player immediately offers an entirely new pose and noise to the player on her right.

When working well this warm up will sound like pairs of matching noises and crazy poses racing around the circle. The player should receive, reflect, turn and create a new pose without pause. It is important that the players not stop to think in between the poses.

Pass Catch is a warm up in silliness. The warm up should help the players accept being silly in front of others. Pass Catch also forces a player into a physical position and to make a noise they would never consider otherwise. This warm up can be used to help players create the move and sound parts of their characters.

Gimmicks

None

Variations

Pass the Peep – The players in the circle try to pass the same pose and noise around the circle

Credits

None

Category: Accepting Characters Problem Warm-Up Tags: circle, silly
exercises.summary: One player creates a silly pose with sound, the next mirrors it exactly then invents a new one to pass right.

exercises.name: Malapropism
exercises.description:
Players mill freely around the room, pointing at objects and loudly calling them something completely different. Free associate with each new object rather than repeating the same words.

  1. Start milling about the space
  2. Point at an object and shout out a random wrong name for it with confidence
  3. Move to the next object and call out something new
  4. Keep energy high and voices loud and clear
  5. Continue until the group loses momentum
exercises.description_raw:
Malapropism

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

Word Game, Disassociation.

Introduction

This warm-up will help us get out of heads, and into the moment for improvisation. Start milling about the room.

Description

As the players are milling about they are to point at objects in the room and call out what the object is not. If they point at a light they may call out “fish”, or “wallet”. The goal is to call out loud and clear like that is what the object is. Watch for people that saying the same thing over and over again. It works better if people free associate with each new object that they point at. The exercise continues until boredom sets in.

None

Variations

Endow the players with accents, emotions or gibberish.

Credits

None

Category: Warm-Up Tags: associations, milling
exercises.summary: Milling players point at objects and shout wildly incorrect names with confident energy and fresh associations.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:
Introduction

This warm-up will help us get out of heads, and into the moment for improvisation. Start milling about the room.

Description

The goal is to call out loud and clear like that is what the object is. Watch for people that saying the same thing over and over again. It works better if people free associate with each new object that they point at. The exercise continues until boredom sets in.

Variations

exercises.name: Leading
exercises.description:
Everyone mills around the space while players call out body parts to lead with, exaggerating movement in silly ways.

  1. Start milling around in open space
  2. One player calls out a body part like left foot, knee, or right ear
  3. All players move leading with that body part
  4. Continue with new body parts called out by different players
  5. Optional: add character sounds and goals to each new movement
exercises.description_raw:
Leading

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

Foot soldiers. Follow Your Nose.

Introduction

This warm-up will get us feeling silly and get the blood moving. Everyone start milling around.

Description

While the players are milling around someone will suggest a body part to lead with. For example if a player yells left foot, all the other players will move with their left foot out front. Continue on with various body parts like: knee, bum, right ear. End the exercise when players start calling out internal organs.

The goal of this warm up is to get players moving in a manner that they would not usually move. Like

With little extension this warm up can become an exercise to explore character creation. In the exercise version of this structure the leader can encourage the players to add noises and goals to the new movement. Leading allows the players to fill out the holy trinity of the character (move, sound and thought) from just changing how they move. The players can also be challenged to return to a previous character when the same body part is called out.

Gimmicks

  • Lead with an internal organ – spleen.
  • Lead with an existential entity – your ego or your Id.

Variations

Endless.

Variations

None

Category: Warm-Up Tags: Ego, Id, milling
exercises.summary: Everyone mills around while players call out body parts to lead with, exaggerating each movement in increasingly silly ways.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:

Variations

exercises.name: Jeepers Peepers
exercises.description:
Get into a tight circle and sync up with quick eye contact moments.

  1. All players look down at their feet
  2. On count of three, look up and make a strong choice to look left, right, or across the circle
  3. If you lock eyes with someone, both let out a brief scream and look back down
  4. For larger groups, players who scream step out and the circle closes while counting 1, 2, 3
  5. Continue until no one remains in the circle
exercises.description_raw:
Jeepers Peepers

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

The Scream. Laughers.

Introduction

This warm-up is designed to try and get us all in sync. Lets get into a tight circle.

Description

The players are organized into a tight circle. The players start by looking down at their feet and avoiding each other’s gaze. On a count of three all the players look up from their feet at once. A player must look left, look right, or look across the circle. The players must make a strong choice of which direction they looking. They should look up as if they are about to look into another player’s eyes. If two players make eye contact, They both let out a brief scream and then return back to looking at their feet.

It should sound like 1, 2, 3, scream, 1, 2, 3, scream. The volume of the scream will vary as random numbers of player will make eye contact. Occasionally it will silent.

If the group is large the two players that scream can step out the circle. The circle closes while counting 1, 2, 3. This competitive version ends when there is no one left to scream with.

This activity is continued until all are out of the circle.

Gimmicks

None

Variations

  • Elimination – Described above.
  • Laughers – Don’t scream let out a laugh.
  • Sing Song – Don’t scream let sing an agreed upon note.

Credits

Seattle Theatresports

Category: Warm-Up Tags: circle, laugh, sing
exercises.summary: Players lock eyes across a shrinking circle, screaming when gazes meet until everyone is eliminated.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:
Introduction

Description

This activity is continued until all are out of the circle.

Variations

  • Elimination – Described above.
  • Laughers – Don’t scream let out a laugh.
  • Sing Song – Don’t scream let sing an agreed upon note.

exercises.name: Hello
exercises.description:
Everyone mills around the room greeting each other face-to-face with "hello." Start with neutral greetings, then the leader adds character endowments to transform how players interact.

  1. Mill about the room freely
  2. Greet each player you meet with a simple "hello," then move on
  3. Leader calls out a character type, emotion, or scenario (angry, tipsy, long-lost friend, plumber, zero gravity, etc.)
  4. Continue milling and greeting with the new endowment affecting your voice and physicality
  5. Repeat with fresh endowments to explore different characters and relationships
exercises.description_raw:
Hello

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

Greetings. Lost Friend.

Introduction

This warm up will help break the ice and get the blood moving. Everyone start to mill about the room.

Description

The players mill about the room. At some point the workshop leader asks them all the greet each other good face to face “hello.” Initially the greeting is kept simple and neutral. Greet one player and move on, greet another and so on. After the salutations are completed the group can return to milling about the room.

The leader can endow the greetings with characters, relationships, vocal types, goals, history, emotions, or occupations. The hellos then become imbued with the endowment. The salutations remain brief and the group returns back to milling about in between

Examples include: anger, fear, love, long lost friend, ex-lovers, debtors, smelly, tipsy, a Scottish Chieftain, plumbers, in zero gravity, a cold cold day, etc. Have fun with it.

This warm up can quickly drift into an exercise that covers characters and relationships. If using Hello as a warm up keep the salutations short and silly. Use Hello as a warm up for breaking the ice. Reminding the players that it is safe to be silly.

When employing as an exercise the salutations are explored a bit longer. Encourage the players to have longer interactions. Keep the content within the realm of the salutation. inquire with players how they chose to move, and sound with the character.

Gimmicks

None

Variations

None.

Credits

None

Category: Warm-Up Tags: characters, milling
exercises.summary: Actors mill around greeting each other with "hello" while adopting character endowments called out by the leader.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:
Introduction

exercises.name: Hands
exercises.description:
Stand in a circle holding hands with players on either side.

  • One player starts by squeezing the hand of the player to their right
  • That player squeezes their right hand to pass it on; if squeezed on the left, squeeze your right hand
  • The squeeze travels around the circle in one direction
  • Leader adds more squeezes going different directions until the circle reaches playful chaos
  • Variations: Gate Controlled (wait for squeeze to complete before passing), Hands Up (push palms overhead instead), Bing Bing Bong (say "bing" for right, "bong" for left)
exercises.description_raw:
Hands

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

Squeezer

Introduction

Everyone into a circle and join hands.

Description

One player in the circle will start by squeezing the hand of the player on her right. That player will squeeze the hand of the player on her right. So if someone squeezes your left hand you squeeze your right hand. This will create a series of squeezes that hurtle around the circle. This is the simplest form of hands.

If a player has their right hand squeezed they will squeeze their left hand. This will cause the squeezes to hurtle in the opposite direction of the paragraph above. So we can have a series of squeezes moving to the right or a series of squeezes moving to the left.

The leader can keep adding squeezes into the circle. There could be a squeeze heading to the right, with another squeeze following behind. There could be a squeeze going to the right and left at the same time. Usually the circle will keep adding squeezes until chaos reigns.

This is an ice breaker warm up that focuses on listening with out words and concentration. Since it usually ends in a chaotic squeezing of sweaty palms Hands also reminds players that failure is okay, and group failure is even more okay.

None.

Variations

  • Gate Controlled – the player cannot start their squeeze until the hand squeezing theirs has completed. This can change the rate of the squeeze moving around the circle.
  • Hands Up – Instead of holding hands the players put their palms up over their heads and push on their neighbour’s hand instead of squeezing. One can watch this push wave travel around the circle.
  • Bing Bing Bong – When a player squeezes their right hand they say bing. When they squeeze their left hand they say bong. One can listen to the wave binging and bonging it’s way around the circle

Credits

Learn Improv made this one up in the 1990s.

Category: Warm-Up Tags: circle
exercises.summary: Squeezes travel around a hand-holding circle, with the leader adding multiple simultaneous pulses until playful chaos erupts.

exercises.name: Group Stop
exercises.description:
Everyone mills about the room while one designated player unexpectedly freezes. The rest of the group must notice and freeze simultaneously, developing collective awareness and synchronization.

  1. All players mill quietly around the room in a loose group
  2. Designated player freezes without warning; others freeze when they notice
  3. Repeat several times, then allow any player to initiate the stop
  4. Progress to using subtext and intuition rather than visual signals to freeze together as one unit
exercises.description_raw:
Group Stop

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

Statues, f-stop

Introduction

This is listening warm-up. Everyone start milling about the room.

Description

The players quietly mill about the room. A designated player will unexpectedly freeze on the spot. The goal is for the rest of players to seamlessly freeze in position as well. Eventually the group will simply freeze together as one unit. When working it is lovely to watch.

After the designated player has stopped the group a few times the warm up moves to the next level. The warm up is mad more challenging by allowing any player can initiate the group stop. With time the players will subtlety work together choosing when to stop as a group.

The leader should keep an eye out and encourage the group to use subtext to group stop in sync. While it is human nature for a group to develop signals for when to stop the most will be learned from Group Stop by using subtext.

Group Stop definitely is a listening warm up. At first it is listening with the eyes, freezing when you see others freeze. At it’s highest level the ensemble will be listening with its heart and brain, knowing when to stop as one.

Gimmicks

  • Endow the players with strange gaits to impair listening
  • Endow the players with loud vocalizations that impair listening

Variations

  • Players make eye contact and greet each other
  • Moving By Numbers – certain number of players move, rest still.

Credits

None

Category: Less Listening Problem Teamwork Warm-Up Tags: endowments, ensemble, listening, milling
exercises.summary: One player freezes without warning while others mill, forcing the group to notice and stop together in synchronized silence.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:
Introduction

This is listening warm-up. Everyone start milling about the room.

Description

The players quietly mill about the room. A designated player will unexpectedly freeze on the spot. The goal is for the rest of players to seamlessly freeze in position as well. Eventually the group will simply freeze together as one unit. When working it is lovely to watch.

After the designated player has stopped the group a few times the warm up moves to the next level. The warm up is mad more challenging by allowing any player can initiate the group stop. With time the players will subtlety work together choosing when to stop as a group.

The leader should keep an eye out and encourage the group to use subtext to group stop in sync. While it is human nature for a group to develop signals for when to stop the most will be learned from Group Stop by using subtext.

Group Stop definitely is a listening warm up. At first it is listening with the eyes, freezing when you see others freeze. At it’s highest level the ensemble will be listening with its heart and brain, knowing when to stop as one.

Gimmicks

  • Endow the players with loud vocalizations that impair listening

Variations

  • Moving By Numbers – certain number of players move, rest still.

exercises.name: Associations
exercises.description:
Players stand in a circle and rapidly call out the first word that comes to mind in response to the previous player's word, without overthinking or judging responses.

  1. Form a circle facing inward
  2. One player starts by saying any word
  3. The next player immediately responds with the first word that comes to mind
  4. Continue around the circle, keeping the pace quick and natural
  5. Let responses flow without pausing to think or edit yourself
exercises.description_raw:
Associations

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

Free Association, Free Association Circle

Introduction

This warm-up will help us get into the moment, and help us feel easier about being silly.

Description

Free Association is a very common warm up in improv comedy. A free association is when a player associates meaning to something without judgement, censorship or thought. It is quite hard to do. For example a player may be exposed to the word “dog” they may free associate “cat.”

There are as many configurations for this warm up as there are free associations. The most common is to organize in a circle and have one player free associate on the previous player’s free association. It should sound like a series of disconnected words. “mouse”, “trap”, “crab”, “grumpy”, “goofy”, “mouse.”

There is no correct answer in free association. Some players will repeat associations, or have long pauses. This is a good opportunity to explore why this is happening. It is a teaching moment where the leader can discuss.

  • There is no need to try and be funny.
  • You are in a non-judgmental space.
  • Allow the first thing in your mind to come out.

This warm up helps with listening and commitment. Many players have editors in their head. Learn Improv calls it the headitor. The headitor keeps a player from using the first thought that an offer evokes. Free association should help a player break free form their headitor.

  • Physical offers – have the player freely associate a physical offer
  • Rhyming offers – have the players associate in rhyme
  • Last Letter – have the player associate with a word that starts with the last letter of the cue word
  • Closed Eye – have players close their eyes to enhance listening

Variations

  • Firing line – one player is hit with many words to free associate
  • Pass the Chi – players complete the second half of a word
  • Cross Circle – next player to free associate is randomly chosen
  • Milling – Players mill about the room free associating freely
  • Disassociation – Say the opposite of the cue word
  • Rhymes – Freely associate but using rhymes
  • Last Letter – Associate a new word using the last letter of cue word
  • Gibberish – Use gibberish to help free from headitor

Credits

The process of free association is credited to Sigmund Fraud

Category: Warm-Up
exercises.summary: Words cascade around the circle as each player instantly responds with the first association that springs to mind.

exercises.name: Flock Dance
exercises.description:
Form a pyramid with one player in front, two behind, three behind them, and so on. The lead player starts a repetitive movement while each row mirrors only the players directly ahead of them, creating natural delay and decay.

  • Lead player begins simple, repetitive movements with music
  • Each row focuses only on mirroring the row in front, not the lead
  • Embrace imperfect copying and the chaos that emerges
  • Vary by having lead turn to face the flock or move too fast to follow
exercises.description_raw:
Flock Dance

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

Aerobics, Conga Line

Introduction

This is a physical warm-up and ice breaker. One player is in front, with two players behind her and three players behind them and so on. You are forming a pyramid.

Description

This warm up is best done with music. The first player starts a repetitive movement or dance. The two players immediately behind the lead player mimic her movements as best as possible. Each row of players follows the row of players ahead of them. Flock Dance works best if the players only try and mimic the players in the line ahead of them. There should be both a delay and a decay in the lead player’s movements.

Flock Dance is a physical warm up that also helps players by getting them to move in uncharacteristic ways. Often players will focus only on the lead player even if they are in the last line. It is important to encourage the players to only follow those directly in front of them. Players can use this as a practise for getting things wrong. Trying to do a perfect Flock Dance defeats the purpose of Flock Dance. Embrace failure, embrace chaos, and let go of being “right.”

  • Lead player turns to face the flock, forcing them to turn around and free style
  • Lead player moves so fast that not even the front line can keep up

Variations

  • Aerobics – there is no layering of the players and everyone copies lead
  • Conga Line – there is a line instead of a flock and the lead player moves about the room

Credits

Thanks to Richard. The safe and supportive man of aerobics

Category: Accepting Listening Problem Warm-Up Tags: characters, circle, failure, line, movement
exercises.summary: One player leads simple movements while each row mirrors only those directly ahead, creating cascading delays and delightful chaos.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:
Introduction

This is a physical warm-up and ice breaker. One player is in front, with two players behind her and three players behind them and so on. You are forming a pyramid.

Description

This warm up is best done with music. The first player starts a repetitive movement or dance. The two players immediately behind the lead player mimic her movements as best as possible. Each row of players follows the row of players ahead of them. Flock Dance works best if the players only try and mimic the players in the line ahead of them. There should be both a delay and a decay in the lead player’s movements.

Flock Dance is a physical warm up that also helps players by getting them to move in uncharacteristic ways. Often players will focus only on the lead player even if they are in the last line. It is important to encourage the players to only follow those directly in front of them. Players can use this as a practise for getting things wrong. Trying to do a perfect Flock Dance defeats the purpose of Flock Dance. Embrace failure, embrace chaos, and let go of being “right.”

  • Lead player turns to face the flock, forcing them to turn around and free style
  • Lead player moves so fast that not even the front line can keep up

Variations

  • Aerobics – there is no layering of the players and everyone copies lead
  • Conga Line – there is a line instead of a flock and the lead player moves about the room

exercises.name: Digits
exercises.description:
Stand in a tight circle with heads lowered and eyes closed. Count to 20 as a group, with each player speaking one number in sequence without coordination.

  • Player one starts by saying "one," then another player says "two," continuing without knowing who will speak next
  • If two players speak simultaneously, restart at "one"
  • Keep attempting until the group successfully reaches "twenty"
  • For variations, substitute the alphabet or build a story one word at a time using the same rules
exercises.description_raw:
Digits

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

Shared counting, Countdown, Synergy, No Doubles.

Introduction

This exercise is designed to get us all in synch. Get into a tight circle.

Description

Everyone in the circle looks down at the ground and closes their eyes. We will try to count to 20 as a group. One player starts at the beginning “one.” Then a different player counts off “two.” With heads lowered and eyes closed no one should know who will speak the next number. If two players speak at the same time then the group must start again at “one.” The group can keep trying until they one player calls out “twenty.” At this point there is much rejoicing.

None.

Variations

  • Alphabet Circle – go through the alphabet instead of numbers
  • Word at a time – If two words come out a the same time story restarts.

Credits

None

Category: Warm-Up Tags: circle, counting, ensemble, numeracy
exercises.summary: The group counts to twenty in a circle with eyes closed, restarting whenever two players speak the same number simultaneously.
exercises.descriptionOriginal:
Introduction

This exercise is designed to get us all in synch. Get into a tight circle.

Description

One player starts at the beginning “one.” Then a different player counts off “two.” With heads lowered and eyes closed no one should know who will speak the next number. If two players speak at the same time then the group must start again at “one.” The group can keep trying until they one player calls out “twenty.” At this point there is much rejoicing.

Variations

  • Alphabet Circle – go through the alphabet instead of numbers
  • Word at a time – If two words come out a the same time story restarts.

exercises.name: Cross Circle
exercises.description:
Players stand in a large circle facing inward and take turns crossing to swap positions by calling out another player's name across the circle.

  1. Player A makes eye contact with someone across the circle, points to them, and calls their name clearly
  2. Player A walks casually across the circle toward that person's spot
  3. The called player (Player B) immediately calls out a different player's name and walks toward their spot
  4. Player A fills the empty space left by Player B, and the pattern continues with constant movement across the circle
  5. Keep names loud and clear so everyone learns them; support any name mistakes with encouragement
exercises.description_raw:
Cross Circle

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

Name Game, Shriner Warm-up

Introduction

This warm up will help us all get moving, listening and learning some names. Let’s form a large circle.

Description

In this warm up players will be crossing the circle to occupy another player’s spot across the circle. They signal who’s spot they are going to occupy by calling our the player’s name. After a player’s name has been called out that player call’s out another players name and moves toward their spot.

If the above explanation worked there will always be a player walking across the circle to a new spot. If the above explanation did not work try the painfully detailed one below.

All the players are arranged in a large circle facing inwards. A player named Eh will randomly pick a player across the circle and call out their name. Let’s presume the player across the circle is named Bee. Player Eh will make eye contact, point towards and then call out “Bee.” Hopefully this will have gotten Bee’s attention.

Player Eh will start to move across the circle towards Bee. Player’s do not run across the circle. Player Eh is going to occupy player Bee’s spot so player Bee has to get moving.

Now player Bee will make eye contact with, point towards and call the name of another player in the circle. Surprisingly this new player’s name is Cee. Bee will start casually moving towards Cee in the circle. Player Eh will now occupy the empty space in the circle left by Bee as she has now started walking towards Cee.

This can continue endlessly until fatigue or hunger sets in

This is a great warm up for learning names. Encourage players to say the names loud and clear so that others can learn names as well. However learning names barely scratches the surface of what this warm up offers. Every aspect of LACE, and more, is demonstrated in cross circle.

Listening. Clearly players must be listening for their names. That is clear enough. There is a lot more to listen for in cross circle. Players can work in listening with their eyes for the initial eye contact. Players can work on listening to the whole stage by learning names of players as others cross the circle. Emotional and subtext listening can be picked up when a player is projecting the angst of not knowing the other player’s name.

Commitment. There is tremendous social pressure to get things right, especially names. Getting players to publicly take risks regarding other players names helps build risk taking behaviour. There is not wrong. Your fellow players will support you. In improv you never know what is next. So stick to your choice loud and proud. If Nancy turns out to be Norma you will be forgiven and we all will learn as a group.

Support. We feel our names are important to us. If someone messes our name up in cross circle we will be supportive. This trains being supportive at all times. Supportive players makes cross circle work better as well.

Focus. Taking it. We deserve the attention of others. If we don’t get it with eye contact we point, and then we call out the player’s name. Focus is also about giving focus. Every player must throw focus to the player

Rules Not Goals. Errors are not corrected they are supported. A successful cross circle is about intent and messing it up is funny. Eventually the group of players will self correct cross circle especially once

Variations

  • High Fives – two players are crossing at once and giving high fives in the center of the circle.
  • Subtle Crossings – remove pointing and player naming rely on just eye contact.
  • Word at a Time – replace the player’s name with a word to create a word at a time story.

Credits

None

Category: Listening Problem Warm-Up Tags: circle, ice breaker, lace, names, walking
exercises.summary: Players call out names and cross the circle to swap positions, creating constant movement while learning each other's names.

exercises.name: Clap Focus
exercises.description:
Stand in a large circle and pass focus through eye contact and clapping. One player makes eye contact with someone across the circle, lunges forward, and claps sharply at them. That player accepts and claps at a different player. Continue building speed until claps fly constantly around the circle.

  1. Form a large circle facing inward
  2. Player one makes eye contact with someone across the circle
  3. Lunge into the circle and clap decisively at them
  4. Selected player accepts and claps at a different player
  5. Keep the rhythm going, building speed and awareness
exercises.description_raw:
Clap Focus

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

Snap Focus. Pass Clap.

Introduction

This warm up is to get players listening, responding and reacting without thinking. Everyone gets into a large circle.

Description

One player in the circle chooses a random player across from them in the circle. They signal the selected player by making eye contact, lunging into the circle and clapping “at them.” It should be really obvious who is has been given the clap. The selected player accepts the offer of the clap and repeats the process with a different player in the circle. This process continues until there are claps flying back and forth across the circle.

Ideally there will be a constant clapping noise. Eventually the players will be so attentive and so quick to respond it sounds like one person clapping rapidly. This warm-up usually ends when people’s hand are denuded of flesh.

One could insert literally any gesture in place of clapping. I have seen nods, toe flicks, middle fingers, hellos, salutes, etc. The two most common versions of this warm up are clap focus and snap focus.

This warm up helps players develop taking focus, and the immediate rewards of making eye contact to connect with other players. On a rudimentary level this is a listening exercise as well.

  • Distractions – those players that are not clapping are snapping their fingers.
  • No Clap – the players do not clap but merely point across the circle to the other player. To make the the warm up a bit more challenging remove any physical cues and move back and forth across the circles with only eye contact.
  • Telepathy – Eyes are closed and players signal each other using their inherent psionic power.

Variations

Snap Focus – Same warm up but snapping fingers instead of clapping.

Credits

Camp camp summer cramp.

Category: Listening Problem Warm-Up Tags: circle, clapping, listening, reactions
exercises.summary: Eye contact and sharp claps ricochet around a circle, building speed and collective focus through reactive listening.

exercises.name: Vampire
exercises.description:
Players mill around the room with eyes closed while spotters stationed on the walls keep them safe. One designated player is the vampire moving among the group. When the vampire grabs another player's wrist, that player screams, opens their eyes, and becomes a spotter. The exercise continues until all players have been caught.

  • Assign multiple spotters to stand on each wall of the room before starting
  • All other players close their eyes and mill slowly around the center, bumping gently into each other
  • Spotters gently redirect players back toward center by touching shoulders; may make spooky sounds
  • The vampire moves among players and grabs a wrist to catch prey, triggering a dramatic scream and eye-opening
  • Caught players become spotters; vampire continues until all are caught
exercises.description_raw:
Vampire

By learnimprov | August 2, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

Screamers, Haunted House.

Introduction

This warm up builds trust amongst players. We need safety players on each wall. Everyone else start slowly milling around the room.

Description

The players on the outside are spotters. Ideally there will be more than four spotters. Remember safety first. Before starting fully explain this warm up to the players. Make it clear that they will feel vulnerable with their eyes closed and they will be unexpectedly touched with out explicit consent. The leader must ensure that players feel safe to not participate. No player needs to explain why they choose not to participate in Vampire (or anything else for that matter).

Instruct the players milling about to close their eyes and continue slowly milling around. It is important to emphasize to move slowly. Advise them that they will be touched in one of three ways. First they will be slowly bumping into each other. They all should be moving slowing enough that this will be safe. The next most likely touch they will receive will be from the spotters on stationed outside the players milling around. This touch will involve the spotters gently redirecting players back into the center of the room by touching their shoulders. The last touch is the most dramatic. If the another player grabs your wrist you have been tapped by the vampire. You will let out an overly dramatic scream.

The leader will select one player to be a vampire. The vampire is milling around with all the other players. However when the vampire nudges into another player she will grab her wrist indicating that the vampire has found some prey. As described above the player who get’s her wrist grabbed will scream, open her eyes and move to being a spotter. The warm up continues until the vampire has eaten every other player.

  • The spotters can make spooky noises as the vampire gets close
  • don’t pick a vampire and let them wander for hours.

Variations

Zombie Walk – Instead of the confidant being a vampire she is a zombie.

Credits

Transylvanian Secretariat of Hematophagic Stereotyping

Category: Problem Teamwork Warm-Up Tags: ensemble, safety, trust
exercises.summary: One player hunts blindfolded movers in the dark until caught players become spotters guiding the remaining prey.

exercises.name: Bippity Bibbity Bopaphant
exercises.description:
Everyone forms a circle with one player spinning in the center. The center player points at someone to "surprise" them, then rapidly yells "bippity bippity bopaphant" while that player and their two neighbors quickly form a three-part elephant: trunk in the middle, floppy ears on both sides, all trumpeting together.

  1. Center player spins, points at a circle player without warning
  2. Pointed player becomes trunk; neighbors on each side become ears
  3. All three must trumpet before center player finishes yelling the phrase
  4. If any part is slow, incomplete, or wrong, that player replaces the center player
  5. Center player decides immediately if unclear who failed
exercises.description_raw:
Bippity Bibbity Bopaphant

By learnimprov | July 17, 2010
0 Comment

Synonyms

Elephant, Bunny, Bippity Bippity Bop

Introduction

Everyone form a circle with one player in the centre. This warm-up breaks the ice, gets people listening and gets the blood flowing.

Description

The player in the centre of the circle starts to spin madly. They may close their eyes, they may count to ten, they may be silent. The goal of the player in the center is to “surprise” one of the players in the circle. The player in the center must make a strong and clear choice about which player in the circle has been “surprised.”

Three players in the circle must quickly form a three part elephant character. The player pointed at must mime the trunk, and the two players on either side of the trunk mime big floppy ears. All three must trumpet like a happy elephant together. This is the simplest description of the elephant. There will be many house variations on how to create the 3 part elephant.

The challenge is to create the 3 part elephant character very quickly. There is not room for indecision. The player in the center of the circle is going to be yelling “bippity bippity bopaphant” as fast as possible. If the 3 part elephant character is not trumpeting (fully created) before the player in the center finishes yelling “bippity bippity bopaphant” someone from the elephant character will be moved to the center.

Typically some part of the elephant will be slow to react. It may be missing a right or left ear, it’s trunk may be flaccid, or someone forgets to trumpet. Whichever player is responsible for this travesty of character development replaces the player in the circle. The circle of life is continued until someone vomits.

Players that jump the gun and create extra ears, or extra trunks or some other mutated elephant appendage also replace the player in the center of the circle. If for some reason there is a tie or it is unclear which player messed up the 3 part elephant character the player in the center decides immediately who replaces her in the center of the circle. If the player in the center is too slow to call out their replacement they will stay in the center.

This is a great listening and commitment warm up. Players must be listening to the cue of being pointed at, as well as listening for when their neighbour has been “surprised.” It is definitely an example of listening with one’s eyes and brain instead of the usual listening for words with your ears. Players must respond so fast to build their 3 part elephant character that there is no room for hesitation. Folks who overthink and worry about how they appear can get some relief with this warm up.

Bippity Bippity Bop is a name with two structures. Let your house decide which you will use, but be aware the there is Bippity Bippity Bop and then there is Bippity Bippity Bop.

Gimmicks

Move from an elephant to a more complicated multi part character. For example a dragon with flaming mouth, two wings and a long tail. Making it asymmetric heightens the level of listening and attention to detail required.

Make up your own house characters: Charlies Angels, Church, Car, Tank, Snake, etc.

Variations

  • Bippity Bippity BunnyBop – Players create a chewing bunny with two tall ears and make a carrot chewing noise. While these variations are endless bunny is very common.
  • Bippity Bippity Bop – No characters involved at all. Just fast talking deceit.

Credits

Summer camps throughout the ages. I also took the liberty of renaming the elephant and bunny subs of Bippity Bippity Bop.

Category: Listening Warm-Up Tags: characters, circle, commitment, ice breaker, listening
exercises.summary: Spin and point to trigger three players forming a coordinated elephant before the phrase ends or swap positions.
